addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Ideas

 Items not yet solid enough for `## Todo` (or surfaced
 during close-out / end-of-day before they're fully
 formed). Triaged at the next Preparation: promote to
 `## Todo` / `notes/todo-backlog.md`, fold into a
 picked-up cycle, or drop.

1. **Codify ochid invariant + bot-repo rules + squash
   gating + cross-repo migration in
   `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.** Was planned for
   `0.59.0-2` but deferred when 0.59.0 closed out via
   squash + Option F (manual bot-side rewrite). The
   rules were exercised manually for the close-out;
   this Idea formally codifies them.
   - Codify the **ochid invariant** in `## ochid
     trailers`: every public ochid must resolve in the
     public graph.
   - Codify bot-repo rules: never squashed; descriptions
     editable (`jj describe` preserves chid).
   - Codify squash gating: until `vc-x1 push --squash`
     exists, manual symmetric squash (Option F: app
     squash + bot-side trailer rewrite + force-push) is
     the standard recipe; merge non-ff is the default
     shape for multi-commit cycles.
   - Sketch cross-repo migration: ochids change at
     every merge until the change reaches the canonical
     repo's `main`.
   - Apply Ideas-aware Preparation/Close-out updates:
     Preparation triages `## Ideas` (promote / fold /
     drop) before declaring the plan; Close-out
     captures unresolved follow-ups into `## Ideas`.
2. **Apply max review #1 + push 0.61.0.** 0.61.0
   close-out is committed locally (`c0f3376f`) but
   **unpushed**; max review #1 lives at [[14]]
   (6 concerns + 4 nits + 1 process observation, each
   with a Disposition entry). Resume by walking items
   with the user; decide apply / drop / defer; apply
   accepted items as audit-doc / copying.md edits;

60	   accepted items as audit-doc / copying.md edits;
61	   push.
62	   - **Open question**: do edits land as an amendment
63	     to `c0f3376f` (unpushed, still mutable via `jj
64	     edit -r c0f3376f`) or as a new commit in a
65	     follow-on cycle? The bot thinks the latter if
66	     non-trivial — Item 1's doc-text rewrite alone
67	     could justify a `0.62.0-0` start, with the
68	     review items + T7 (`validate-desc`/`fix-desc`
69	     equalization) as the cycle's picked-up work.
70	   - **Push shape**: user stated intent at EoD
71	     2026-05-26 is **merge non-ff** for 0.61.0. Recipe
72	     (per [`cycle-protocol.md > Shape at close-out
73	     push`](cycle-protocol.md#shape-at-close-out-push)):
74	     `jj rebase -r <tip=c0f3376f> --onto
75	     <prev=41ef884> --onto <sub-tip=587749d>`, then
76	     manual `jj git push` (the `vc-x1 push` wrapper
77	     only fully supports keep-separate today).
78	   - **Working-copy state at EoD**: app `@` is empty
79	     on `c0f3376f`; `.claude` accumulates session data
80	     (commits at push time per the `.claude` cadence
81	     rule). One uncommitted edit: this Ideas entry
82	     itself in `todo.md`.
83	
84	## Priorities
85	
86	- P1 is highest priority, same priority are grouped equally
87	- A line with `...` is a prefix-match against a `## Todo` title
88	  - resolves locally (in this file); should be unique
89	- Entries without a prefix-match are free-form
90	  - chores tasks, ad-hoc reminders.
91	
92	### P1
93	
94	- `**vc-x1 push: support...**`
95	- `**vc-x1 push --squash...**`
96	
97	### P2
98	
99	- `**single-field...**`
100	
101	### P3
102	
103	- `**por -> dual...**`
104	
105	## Todo
106	
107	 Prioritized entries (referenced by `## Priorities`) lower priority
108	 todo's are in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). In all cases
109	 we use the [Prose Form in CLAUDE.md](/CLAUDE.md#prose-form). When
110	 more detail is waranted those reside in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`.
111	 Also, we use the  design subsections (link via `[N]` ref). Run
112	 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
113	
114	1. **vc-x1 push: support new cycle protocol shape (N:1 code↔bot).**
115	   Today push assumes 1:1 symmetric WC commits with shared
116	   title/body. The new cycle protocol has a different shape on
117	   each side:
118	   - code side: fully committed before push (N commits via
119	     merge), nothing to commit at push time
120	   - `.claude` side: one commit with its own message (distinct
121	     from any code commit) and a multi-line `ochid:` listing
122	     all N code commits
123	
124	   Teach push to:
125	   - detect this shape
126	   - skip `commit-app` when code WC is empty
127	   - compose a `.claude`-specific message
128	   - emit multi-line `ochid:` per the design in [[10]]
129	
130	   Today's workaround: pre-commit `.claude` manually, then
131	   `vc-x1 push <bm> --from bookmark-both --yes`.
132	2. **vc-x1 push --squash: symmetric squash on both repos.**
133	   Automate Option F (manually exercised in 0.59.0
134	   close-out): app-side squash + bot-side description
135	   rewrite + force-push, atomically. Without this,
136	   squash is a manual recipe that future cycles must
137	   follow each time.
138	   - App side: squash cycle commits into one new commit;
139	     capture the squashed chid.

 more detail is waranted those reside in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`.
 Also, we use the  design subsections (link via `[N]` ref). Run

1. **vc-x1 push: support new cycle protocol shape (N:1 code↔bot).**
   Today push assumes 1:1 symmetric WC commits with shared
   title/body. The new cycle protocol has a different shape on
   each side:
   - code side: fully committed before push (N commits via
     merge), nothing to commit at push time
   - `.claude` side: one commit with its own message (distinct
     from any code commit) and a multi-line `ochid:` listing
     all N code commits

   Teach push to:
   - detect this shape
   - skip `commit-app` when code WC is empty
   - compose a `.claude`-specific message
   - emit multi-line `ochid:` per the design in [[10]]

   Today's workaround: pre-commit `.claude` manually, then
   `vc-x1 push <bm> --from bookmark-both --yes`.
2. **vc-x1 push --squash: symmetric squash on both repos.**
   Automate Option F (manually exercised in 0.59.0
   close-out): app-side squash + bot-side description
   rewrite + force-push, atomically. Without this,
   squash is a manual recipe that future cycles must
   follow each time.
   - App side: squash cycle commits into one new commit;
     capture the squashed chid.

- **Most pressing — Ideas #2**: apply max review #1 ([notes/design-cli/max-review-1.md](notes/design-cli/max-review-1.md), 6 concerns + 4 nits + 1 process observation, each with Disposition) and push 0.61.0. Open question: amend `c0f3376f` vs. start `0.62.0` cycle. Push shape is **merge non-ff**.

message.content: So I want to continue the critique of the changes for a new desing in 0.61.0, the critique is in notes/design-cli/max-review-1.md

content.content:
1	# Max review #1 — 0.61.0 por/dual parity design
2	
3	Captured 2026-05-26 (max effort) after the close-out commit
4	of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
5	review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
6	chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
7	open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
8	disposition), or applied (already changed).
9	
10	## Scope reviewed
11	
12	- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
13	  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).
14	- [`copying.md`](copying.md) — copying mechanism design
15	  stub from `0.61.0-4`.
16	- [`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md) — older
17	  forward-looking doc (read for context only; not
18	  critiqued).
19	- [`/notes/chores/chores-12.md > ## docs: por/dual parity
20	  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
21	  cycle narrative.
22	
23	Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
24	close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
25	durable references.
26	
27	## Strengths
28	
29	For balance — what the bot thinks works well:
30	
31	- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality
32	  inversion → axis decomposition). The `-2` inversion is
33	  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
34	  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
35	  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
36	- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling
37	  out `--por` as a bundle of six axes opens design space
38	  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
39	  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
40	  a real defect of the bundle framing.
41	- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
42	  initially listed as independent; the design pass
43	  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
44	  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
45	- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
46	  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
47	  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
48	  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
49	  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
50	  elegantly.
51	- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
52	  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
53	  concrete equalization that validates the
54	  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
55	- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
56	  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
57	  itself for in 0.65.0.
58	
59	## Concerns
60	
61	### 1. Runtime `--por` semantics — doc text vs. intent
62	
63	**Original critique** (max, 2026-05-26): The Topology
64	Decisions block at `audit.md:530-534` reads:
65	
66	> `--por` at runtime short-circuits `default_scope` →
67	> **`Scope([Code])`** regardless of what the workspace
68	> says. Escape hatch for "this command, just my code,
69	> leave `.claude/` alone."
70	
71	The bot read that as code-privileged: creation-time
72	`--por` means "this workspace is single," runtime `--por`
73	means "ignore the bot side this invocation." Two
74	different operations mounted on one spelling — felt
75	overloaded.
76	
77	**User clarification** (wink, 2026-05-26): runtime `--por`
78	isn't side-specific. It means **"treat the repo I'm
79	pointed at (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as a
80	single repo — no sibling lookup."** Symmetric across
81	code and bot:
82	
83	- `--por -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate on code,
84	  no sibling.
85	- `--por -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`, no sibling.
86	- `--por -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate on that
87	  repo as-is.
88	
89	Creation-time `--por` is a special case of the same rule
90	(the target doesn't exist yet, so "treat as standalone"
91	collapses to "create as standalone"). One rule, two life
92	stages.
93	
94	**Bot retraction:** the "two meanings on one spelling"
95	critique evaporates under this framing. Also: the bot's
96	proposed alternative ("use `--scope code` instead") was
97	wrong — it conflates *selecting one side of a known dual
98	workspace* (`--scope`) with *declaring the target isn't
99	dual* (`--por`). Different mental models, not
100	interchangeable.
101	
102	**Status:** **resolved on framing; doc-text update
103	pending.** The audit doc currently codifies the narrower
104	code-asymmetric reading at two spots:
105	
106	- `audit.md:530-534` (Topology Decisions, "runtime
107	  override" bullet) — says `→ Scope([Code])`, "just my
108	  code."
109	- `audit.md:897-913` (Por's view of the chain, "Runtime
110	  `--por`" bullet) — says "short-circuits
111	  `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` and ignores
112	  `.claude/`."
113	
114	**Action:** at the next pickup of this design (likely
115	0.62.0 Preparation), rewrite both spots to spell out the
116	target-relative semantics. Suggested text for the
117	Topology bullet:
118	
119	> Runtime override on every subcommand: `--por` declares
120	> the target repo (default `.`, or `-R/--repo <path>`)
121	> as single — no sibling traversal, no `.vc-config.toml
122	> > other-repo` lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation.
123	> Symmetric across code and bot — `--por` doesn't
124	> privilege a side, it suppresses the sibling-discovery
125	> step.
126	
127	### 2. "Exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" is contradictory phrasing
128	
129	**Critique:** `audit.md:521-522` says clap-enforced
130	exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual`; `:525` says
131	default-dual. If neither flag is passed, either clap
132	rejects (true exactly-one-of, friction for the common
133	case) or it accepts (default kicks in, "exactly-one-of"
134	is misleading). Same shape on `--private` / `--public`
135	at `:643-647`.
136	
137	**Status:** open.
138	
139	**Proposed action:** spell it "**at most one of**;
140	absence → workspace-config or baked-in default." Honest
141	about what the default does. Same fix for Privacy. Docs-
142	only edit, no code implications.
143	
144	### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability
145	
146	**Critique:** `copying.md:29-38` defines six flags:
147	`--init-from`, `--init-from-code`, `--init-from-bot`,
148	and `-recursive` variants of each. But recursion is a
149	property of the *operand*, not the flag: `cp src/* dst/`
150	is recursive whether you said `-r` or not; `cp
151	src/foo.txt dst/` isn't. The shell expands the glob; the
152	bot thinks vc-x1 should walk each resolved path and do
153	the right thing.
154	
155	**Proposed action:** one flag per side.
156	`--init-from-code=<glob>` does the right thing per
157	resolved path (file → file copy; dir → recursive copy).
158	Cuts the surface in half, matches `cp` semantics users
159	already have in their head. The `-recursive` naming
160	muddies what the flag controls vs. what the operand
161	controls.
162	
163	**Status:** open. Material to copying.md design before
164	that cycle starts.
165	
166	### 4. `--repo none` × Topology interaction isn't spelled out
167	
168	**Critique:** the Remote axis Decisions
169	(`audit.md:596-624`) introduces `--repo none` as a peer
170	category, with `--account` skipped under it. Fine in
171	isolation. But `--repo none` × `--dual` means "dual
172	workspace with no remote for either side." The
173	bot/sync/push workflow assumes remote-backed `.claude/`.
174	The doc doesn't say whether dual+no-remote is
175	first-class or rejected at parse time.
176	
177	**Proposed action:** add one paragraph to the Remote
178	axis (or matrix footnotes) on the Topology × Remote=none
179	interaction. Don't leave it implicit. The bot thinks a
180	future user trying `init --dual --repo none` will hit a
181	confused error later in the workflow.
182	
183	**Status:** open. Affects implementation when `--repo
184	none` lands (Gap #5).
185	
186	### 5. List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" semantics deserves an explicit callout
187	
188	**Critique:** `audit.md:697-699` says: "If user passes
189	any `--init-from*` on CLI, the config-pinned list is
190	ignored — match the broader chain rule."
191	
192	The "broader chain rule" is about *scalar* values where
193	"wins" unambiguously means "replaces." For list-valued
194	axes, the bot thinks users coming from `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`
195	/ `PATH` will expect merge, not replace. The replace
196	choice is defensible (predictability beats expressivity)
197	but it's not the obvious choice.
198	
199	**Proposed action:** keep the replace semantics, but add
200	an explicit "list-typed-axis rule" sentence — "for
201	list-valued axes like Copying, CLI replaces config; to
202	merge, pass the config globs on CLI too." Prevents the
203	same conversation in 0.62.0.
204	
205	**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.
206	
207	### 6. Gap-list ordering hides one structural dependency
208	
209	**Critique:** `audit.md:1010-1080` ranks gaps by "blast
210	radius." But Gap #7 (`default_scope` broken-dual
211	detection) is a *prerequisite* for Gap #9 (copying
212	mechanism), per `copying.md:81-92` — copying defers
213	validation to the first downstream subcommand, which
214	means broken-dual detection has to exist first.
215	
216	**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
217	"ranking is rough; some structural prerequisites (e.g.
218	#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
219	renumbering.
220	
221	**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.
222	
223	## Nits
224	
225	Lower-priority items; do as time allows.
226	
227	- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
228	  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
229	  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
230	  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
231	  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
232	  hardening a use case nobody asked for.
233	
234	- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
235	  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
236	  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
237	  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
238	  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.
239	
240	- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
241	  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
242	  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
243	  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
244	  get consulted?") — a different angle.
245	
246	- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
247	  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
248	  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
249	  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.
250	
251	## Process observation
252	
253	The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
254	toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
255	close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
256	`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
257	initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
258	was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
259	delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
260	a more thorough process.
261	
262	The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
263	pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
264	correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
265	ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
266	emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
267	of the original user framing would balance the record.
268	
269	**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
270	action implied for the design itself).
271	
272	## Disposition table
273	
274	| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
275	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
276	| 1 | Runtime `--por` semantics — doc text vs. intent | Resolved on framing; doc-text update pending | `audit.md` Topology Decisions + "Por's view" subsections |
277	| 2 | "Exactly-one-of with default" phrasing | Open | `audit.md` Topology + Privacy Decisions |
278	| 3 | `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface | Open | `copying.md` Surface section |
279	| 4 | `--repo none` × Topology interaction | Open | `audit.md` Remote Decisions or matrix footnote |
280	| 5 | List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" | Open | `audit.md` Copying Decisions |
281	| 6 | Gap-list ordering hides one prereq | Open | `audit.md` Gap list intro |
282	| N1 | `finalize` matrix row footnote | Open | `audit.md` matrix |
283	| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | Open | `audit.md` top |
284	| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | Open | `audit.md` § "Por's view" |
285	| N4 | `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` topology-blind note | Open | `audit.md` (anywhere appropriate) |
286	| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |
287	
288	## TL;DR
289	
290	Items 1 and 3 are the bot's highest-conviction concerns —
291	the runtime `--por` semantics doc (now resolved on
292	framing but doc text still needs the rewrite) and the
293	`--init-from-recursive` surface doubling. The rest are
294	docs-only edits that don't block 0.62.0 starting on T7,
295	but each is cheaper to apply now (single design pass)
296	than after the next ten cycles have referenced the
297	current text.
298

# Max review #1 — 0.61.0 por/dual parity design

Captured 2026-05-26 (max effort) after the close-out commit
of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
disposition), or applied (already changed).

## Scope reviewed

- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).
- [`copying.md`](copying.md) — copying mechanism design
  stub from `0.61.0-4`.
- [`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md) — older
  forward-looking doc (read for context only; not
  critiqued).
- [`/notes/chores/chores-12.md > ## docs: por/dual parity
  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
  cycle narrative.

Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
durable references.

## Strengths

For balance — what the bot thinks works well:

- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality
  inversion → axis decomposition). The `-2` inversion is
  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling
  out `--por` as a bundle of six axes opens design space
  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
  a real defect of the bundle framing.
- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
  initially listed as independent; the design pass
  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
  elegantly.
- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
  concrete equalization that validates the
  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
  itself for in 0.65.0.

## Concerns

### 1. Runtime `--por` semantics — doc text vs. intent

**Original critique** (max, 2026-05-26): The Topology
Decisions block at `audit.md:530-534` reads:

> `--por` at runtime short-circuits `default_scope` →
> **`Scope([Code])`** regardless of what the workspace
> says. Escape hatch for "this command, just my code,
> leave `.claude/` alone."

The bot read that as code-privileged: creation-time
`--por` means "this workspace is single," runtime `--por`
means "ignore the bot side this invocation." Two
different operations mounted on one spelling — felt
overloaded.

**User clarification** (wink, 2026-05-26): runtime `--por`
isn't side-specific. It means **"treat the repo I'm
pointed at (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as a
single repo — no sibling lookup."** Symmetric across
code and bot:

- `--por -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate on code,
  no sibling.
- `--por -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`, no sibling.
- `--por -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate on that
  repo as-is.

Creation-time `--por` is a special case of the same rule
(the target doesn't exist yet, so "treat as standalone"
collapses to "create as standalone"). One rule, two life
stages.

**Bot retraction:** the "two meanings on one spelling"
critique evaporates under this framing. Also: the bot's
proposed alternative ("use `--scope code` instead") was
wrong — it conflates *selecting one side of a known dual
workspace* (`--scope`) with *declaring the target isn't
dual* (`--por`). Different mental models, not
interchangeable.

**Status:** **resolved on framing; doc-text update
pending.** The audit doc currently codifies the narrower
code-asymmetric reading at two spots:

- `audit.md:530-534` (Topology Decisions, "runtime
  override" bullet) — says `→ Scope([Code])`, "just my
  code."
- `audit.md:897-913` (Por's view of the chain, "Runtime
  `--por`" bullet) — says "short-circuits
  `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` and ignores
  `.claude/`."

**Action:** at the next pickup of this design (likely
0.62.0 Preparation), rewrite both spots to spell out the
target-relative semantics. Suggested text for the
Topology bullet:

> Runtime override on every subcommand: `--por` declares
> the target repo (default `.`, or `-R/--repo <path>`)
> as single — no sibling traversal, no `.vc-config.toml
> > other-repo` lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation.
> Symmetric across code and bot — `--por` doesn't
> privilege a side, it suppresses the sibling-discovery
> step.

### 2. "Exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" is contradictory phrasing

**Critique:** `audit.md:521-522` says clap-enforced
exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual`; `:525` says
default-dual. If neither flag is passed, either clap
rejects (true exactly-one-of, friction for the common
case) or it accepts (default kicks in, "exactly-one-of"
is misleading). Same shape on `--private` / `--public`
at `:643-647`.

**Status:** open.

**Proposed action:** spell it "**at most one of**;
absence → workspace-config or baked-in default." Honest
about what the default does. Same fix for Privacy. Docs-
only edit, no code implications.

### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability

**Critique:** `copying.md:29-38` defines six flags:
`--init-from`, `--init-from-code`, `--init-from-bot`,
and `-recursive` variants of each. But recursion is a
property of the *operand*, not the flag: `cp src/* dst/`
is recursive whether you said `-r` or not; `cp
src/foo.txt dst/` isn't. The shell expands the glob; the
bot thinks vc-x1 should walk each resolved path and do
the right thing.

**Proposed action:** one flag per side.
`--init-from-code=<glob>` does the right thing per
resolved path (file → file copy; dir → recursive copy).
Cuts the surface in half, matches `cp` semantics users
already have in their head. The `-recursive` naming
muddies what the flag controls vs. what the operand
controls.

**Status:** open. Material to copying.md design before
that cycle starts.

### 4. `--repo none` × Topology interaction isn't spelled out

**Critique:** the Remote axis Decisions
(`audit.md:596-624`) introduces `--repo none` as a peer
category, with `--account` skipped under it. Fine in
isolation. But `--repo none` × `--dual` means "dual
workspace with no remote for either side." The
bot/sync/push workflow assumes remote-backed `.claude/`.
The doc doesn't say whether dual+no-remote is
first-class or rejected at parse time.

**Proposed action:** add one paragraph to the Remote
axis (or matrix footnotes) on the Topology × Remote=none
interaction. Don't leave it implicit. The bot thinks a
future user trying `init --dual --repo none` will hit a
confused error later in the workflow.

**Status:** open. Affects implementation when `--repo
none` lands (Gap #5).

### 5. List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" semantics deserves an explicit callout

**Critique:** `audit.md:697-699` says: "If user passes
any `--init-from*` on CLI, the config-pinned list is
ignored — match the broader chain rule."

The "broader chain rule" is about *scalar* values where
"wins" unambiguously means "replaces." For list-valued
axes, the bot thinks users coming from `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`
/ `PATH` will expect merge, not replace. The replace
choice is defensible (predictability beats expressivity)
but it's not the obvious choice.

**Proposed action:** keep the replace semantics, but add
an explicit "list-typed-axis rule" sentence — "for
list-valued axes like Copying, CLI replaces config; to
merge, pass the config globs on CLI too." Prevents the
same conversation in 0.62.0.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

### 6. Gap-list ordering hides one structural dependency

**Critique:** `audit.md:1010-1080` ranks gaps by "blast
radius." But Gap #7 (`default_scope` broken-dual
detection) is a *prerequisite* for Gap #9 (copying
mechanism), per `copying.md:81-92` — copying defers
validation to the first downstream subcommand, which
means broken-dual detection has to exist first.

**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
"ranking is rough; some structural prerequisites (e.g.
#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
renumbering.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

## Nits

Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
  hardening a use case nobody asked for.

- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.

- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
  get consulted?") — a different angle.

- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Runtime `--por` semantics — doc text vs. intent | Resolved on framing; doc-text update pending | `audit.md` Topology Decisions + "Por's view" subsections |
| 2 | "Exactly-one-of with default" phrasing | Open | `audit.md` Topology + Privacy Decisions |
| 3 | `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface | Open | `copying.md` Surface section |
| 4 | `--repo none` × Topology interaction | Open | `audit.md` Remote Decisions or matrix footnote |
| 5 | List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" | Open | `audit.md` Copying Decisions |
| 6 | Gap-list ordering hides one prereq | Open | `audit.md` Gap list intro |
| N1 | `finalize` matrix row footnote | Open | `audit.md` matrix |
| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | Open | `audit.md` top |
| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | Open | `audit.md` § "Por's view" |
| N4 | `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` topology-blind note | Open | `audit.md` (anywhere appropriate) |
| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |

## TL;DR

Items 1 and 3 are the bot's highest-conviction concerns —
the runtime `--por` semantics doc (now resolved on
framing but doc text still needs the rewrite) and the
`--init-from-recursive` surface doubling. The rest are
docs-only edits that don't block 0.62.0 starting on T7,
but each is cheaper to apply now (single design pass)
than after the next ten cycles have referenced the
current text.

# References

[1]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md
[2]: /notes/design-cli/copying.md
[3]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity.md
[4]: /notes/chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610

505	
506	#### Topology
507	
508	- **States** — `single` (one code repo) | `dual` (code repo
509	  + `.claude/` companion cross-linked by `ochid:`).
510	- **Today** — dual is the implicit default; `--por`
511	  is the boolean opt-out. No `--dual` peer.
512	- **Surface** — `--por` (boolean, `init` and `clone` only).
513	  Downstream commands infer from `.vc-config.toml >
514	  [workspace] other-repo` via `default_scope`.
515	- **Gap** — `--dual` doesn't exist as a peer flag, so the
516	  defaulting is asymmetric: you can be explicit about por
517	  but not about dual.
518	
519	**Decisions (0.61.0):**
520	
521	- **Add `--dual` peer flag**. `--por` / `--dual`
522	  There are two possible values --por or --dual
523	  --por is Plan Old Repo and when used the target
524	  is treated as a regular repo.
525	- **Default: `dual`** 
526	  Dual is the primary use case; defaulting to it matches
527	  the primary use case and the reason vc-x1 exists.
528	- **Runtime override on every subcommand** (not just
529	  `init` / `clone`). `--por` at runtime short-circuits
530	  `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` regardless of what
531	  the workspace says.
532	- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml` is dual-only**. Por
533	  never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
534	  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` gets
535	  dropped.
536	- **Resolution chain**: CLI > ENV > Local > Global >
537	  baked-in default (`dual`). No "Error" floor for
538	  Topology — baked-in always supplies.
539	
540	#### `.vc-config.toml` write — collapsed
541	
542	This axis was originally written as independent. After
543	the Topology decisions, **it collapses** — presence of
544	the workspace `.vc-config.toml` is perfectly correlated
545	with Topology's choice:
546	
547	- Topology=por → no `.vc-config.toml` (por never reads
548	  or writes the workspace file).
549	- Topology=dual → `.vc-config.toml` is mandatory (it's
550	  what detects dual at runtime).
551	
552	This is **not an independent axis**.
553	
554	The capability `--config <path>` was meant to serve
555	(custom workspace metadata, arbitrary file copy) is
556	deferred to a broader **copying** design — see
557	[`notes/design-cli/copying.md`](copying.md) [[3]]. That design uses
558	`--init-from-code` / `--init-from-bot` flags to copy
559	arbitrary files (including `.vc-config.toml` and
560	`.gitignore`), suppresses canned writes when engaged,
561	and defers the "is this dual workspace functional?"
562	check to the first downstream subcommand.
563

#### Topology

- **States** — `single` (one code repo) | `dual` (code repo
  + `.claude/` companion cross-linked by `ochid:`).
- **Today** — dual is the implicit default; `--por`
  is the boolean opt-out. No `--dual` peer.
- **Surface** — `--por` (boolean, `init` and `clone` only).
  Downstream commands infer from `.vc-config.toml >
  [workspace] other-repo` via `default_scope`.
- **Gap** — `--dual` doesn't exist as a peer flag, so the
  defaulting is asymmetric: you can be explicit about por
  but not about dual.

**Decisions (0.61.0):**

- **Add `--dual` peer flag**. `--por` / `--dual`
  There are two possible values --por or --dual
  --por is Plan Old Repo and when used the target
  is treated as a regular repo.
- **Default: `dual`** 
  Dual is the primary use case; defaulting to it matches
  the primary use case and the reason vc-x1 exists.
- **Runtime override on every subcommand** (not just
  `init` / `clone`). `--por` at runtime short-circuits
  `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` regardless of what
  the workspace says.
- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml` is dual-only**. Por
  never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` gets
  dropped.
- **Resolution chain**: CLI > ENV > Local > Global >
  baked-in default (`dual`). No "Error" floor for
  Topology — baked-in always supplies.

#### `.vc-config.toml` write — collapsed

This axis was originally written as independent. After
the Topology decisions, **it collapses** — presence of
the workspace `.vc-config.toml` is perfectly correlated
with Topology's choice:

- Topology=por → no `.vc-config.toml` (por never reads
  or writes the workspace file).
- Topology=dual → `.vc-config.toml` is mandatory (it's
  what detects dual at runtime).

This is **not an independent axis**.

The capability `--config <path>` was meant to serve
(custom workspace metadata, arbitrary file copy) is
deferred to a broader **copying** design — see
[`notes/design-cli/copying.md`](copying.md) [[3]]. That design uses
`--init-from-code` / `--init-from-bot` flags to copy
arbitrary files (including `.vc-config.toml` and
`.gitignore`), suppresses canned writes when engaged,
and defers the "is this dual workspace functional?"
check to the first downstream subcommand.

content.text:
The contradiction's gone — that resolves the core of Concern #2. A few thoughts:

- **Typo**: "Plan Old Repo" → "Plain Old Repo" (POR is Plain Old, like POJO/POCO/POD).
- **Asymmetric definition**: the new text defines `--por` ("target treated as a regular repo") but not `--dual`. If we're spelling out one, spell out both for parity.
- **What "absence" does is now implicit, not stated.** The old text was contradictory (exactly-one-of + default-dual); the new text drops the contradiction by deleting both claims. The "Default: `dual`" bullet implies the answer, but the review's proposed action ("**at most one of**; absence → workspace-config or baked-in default") spelled it out. Worth deciding: leave implicit, or say it explicitly?
- **Prose form drift** (per [CLAUDE.md > Prose form](../../CLAUDE.md#prose-form)): the surrounding Decisions block is one-line bullets with optional indented continuations. The new "Add `--dual` peer flag" bullet has three lines of running prose tacked on without two-space indent, so it reads as a paragraph break rather than continuation. Same for "Default: `dual`". Easy fix — reflow as `- **X**.\n  <indented continuation>`.
- **Concern #1 doc-text** (the `Scope([Code])` runtime-override bullet at 528-531) is untouched, which matches the review's disposition — just confirming you haven't started that one.

Want me to apply the typo + parity + prose-form fixes inline?

Better — typo fixed, `--dual` defined for parity, sub-bullets give the structure. Remaining nits:

- **Sub-bullet indent is 3 spaces; should be 2.** Look at the existing pattern at lines 510-511: parent `- **Today**` continuation is 2-space indented. The new sub-bullets `   - --por` / `   - --dual` use 3, so they don't align with the file's convention. And the `--por` sub-bullet's *own* continuation at line 524 is at col 2 (`  is treated...`), which should align under the sub-bullet's text (col 5), like the `--dual` continuation at line 526 (`     whose default name`).
- **Missing backticks** on `--por`, `--dual`, `.claude` inside the sub-bullets. Rest of the file is consistent about code-spanning these.
- **Redundant intro line**: "There are two possible values --por or --dual." just restates what the two sub-bullets that follow show. Could drop.
- **Terminology drift**: line 523 calls it "regular repo"; line 508 already established "single (one code repo)". Using "single" keeps the doc's vocabulary consistent.
- **"nested"** in the `--dual` definition is filesystem-true (`.claude/` lives inside the code working tree) but the established framing in this doc is "companion cross-linked by `ochid:`" (line 509). "Nested" reads as "a sub-repo of," which understates the cross-link relationship. Minor — flag for your call.
- **Trailing whitespace** on lines 526 and 527.
- **"Absence → default" still implicit.** "Default: `dual`" implies it; the review's proposed wording made it explicit. Your call whether to spell it out — both are defensible.

Proposed shape:

```
- **Add `--dual` peer flag** — two values, `--por` | `--dual`:
  - `--por` (Plain Old Repo): target is treated as a single
    repo.
  - `--dual`: a code repo with a `.claude/` companion bot
    repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`.
- **Default: `dual`** — dual is the primary use case and the
  reason vc-x1 exists. Absence of either flag → `dual` (via
  the resolution chain below).
```

Want me to apply that, or do you want to keep iterating on the wording?

898	
899	- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml`** (layer 3) — dual-only.
900	  Por never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
901	  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` is residue

903	  should be dropped.

905	  (layer 4) — topology-neutral. Both por and dual consult
906	  it.
907	- **Runtime `--por`** — overrides workspace topology
908	  detection across every subcommand (not just
909	  `init`/`clone`). In a dual workspace, runtime `--por`
910	  short-circuits `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` and
911	  ignores `.claude/`.

Precedent in the codebase: `config.rs:113` already honors
`XDG_CONFIG_HOME`; `test_tmp_root.rs` uses
`VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR` / `VC_X1_TEST_KEEP`. The pattern is
established; `VC_X1_*` just extends it.

#### Por's view of the chain

Following the decisions in this cycle:

- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml`** (layer 3) — dual-only.
  Por never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` is residue
  from when `--por` was a half-implemented opt-out and
  should be dropped.
- **User-config `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`**
  (layer 4) — topology-neutral. Both por and dual consult
  it.
- **Runtime `--por`** — overrides workspace topology
  detection across every subcommand (not just
  `init`/`clone`). In a dual workspace, runtime `--por`
  short-circuits `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` and
  ignores `.claude/`.

### Subcommand × parameter matrix

The designed-state at-a-glance view — for each
subcommand (rows), which parameter families it accepts
(columns), and a short note on what the parameter does
in that context.

This is the **post-equalization target**, not today's
state. Today many of these cells are gaps (per the audit
findings); the matrix captures what 0.62.0+ equalization
cycles drive toward.

content.text:
Two spots are flagged; here's what I suggest for each.

**Spot 1** — `audit.md:529-532` (Topology Decisions, "Runtime override" bullet). Keep it tight, matches the surrounding bullets:

```
- **Runtime override on every subcommand** (not just
  `init` / `clone`). `--por` declares the target repo
  (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single —
  no sibling traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo`
  lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric
  across code and bot — `--por` suppresses sibling
  discovery for whichever repo is the target, it
  doesn't privilege a side.
```

**Spot 2** — `audit.md:907-911` ("Por's view of the chain"). This subsection is explanatory rather than decisive, so examples earn their space here:

```
- **Runtime `--por`** — overrides workspace topology
  detection across every subcommand (not just
  `init`/`clone`). Treats the target repo (`.` by
  default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single: no sibling
  traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo` lookup, no
  cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric across code
  and bot:
  - `--por -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate on
    code, no sibling.
  - `--por -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`, no
    sibling.
  - `--por -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate on that
    repo as-is.
```

**Why this split**: Decisions blocks elsewhere in this section are 3-5 line bullets without nested examples — keeping Spot 1 in that shape preserves scannability. The "Por's view" subsection is the natural home for the concrete examples that make the symmetry click.

One question before I apply: should I also grep the audit doc for other spots that codify the old asymmetric reading (e.g. anywhere else that says "`Scope([Code])`" or "ignore `.claude/`")? Two spots were called out in the review; there might be a third I'd want to catch in the same pass.

modify it.

**Topology column (`T`) — quick reference:**

- *Creation* (init/clone): `--por` / `--dual` chooses
  the workspace shape that gets written into
  `.vc-config.toml` (dual only — por writes nothing).
- *Runtime override* (other subcommands): `--por` at
  invocation short-circuits `default_scope` →
  `Scope([Code])` regardless of workspace contents.
  Default-dual still applies at the workspace level
  (the `.vc-config.toml` says dual, but the user is
  asking for "this command only, just my code").
- `finalize` accepts T as a runtime override and SC for
  per-side selection. Today's only use site is inside
  `push`'s workflow against a specific repo path, but
  the body itself is generic (squash + commit with
  title/body + push) and works on either side
  standalone — `--scope=code` or `--scope=bot`. Use
  case: a user manually squashing a cycle into one
  commit and pushing. `--squash` is logically optional
  (auto-detect from `@` being empty); `--detach` and
  `--delay` are unnecessary outside `push`'s schedule-
  detached flow but still work.

   `--account` skipped when `--repo none` resolves.

   removed. Por never reads, never creates the workspace
   metadata file.

   silent fallback to `Scope([Code])` when `.vc-config.toml`
   is missing in a `.claude/`-present workspace is a
   footgun. Add explicit broken-dual error. Prerequisite
   for the copying design's deferred-validation flow.
8. **Runtime `--por` on all subcommands** — add the flag
   to every subcommand (not just `init`/`clone`); thread

file.content:
- Files touched:
  - `src/sync.rs:17,160-169` (`resolve_params_to_repos`).
  - `src/common.rs:569-653` (`find_workspace_root`,
    `default_scope`, `scope_to_repos`).
  - `src/sync/integration_tests.rs` (whole file).
- Divergence: Sync is the one place por is a first-class
  case at runtime. `default_scope` (common.rs:604-616)
  returns `Scope([Code])` when there is no workspace root
  (POR) or no `workspace.other-repo` key; returns
  `Scope([Code, Bot])` only when the dual marker is
  present. `scope_to_repos` (common.rs:626-653) resolves
  `Side::Code` to cwd's `.` when `workspace_root` is None,
  so a por run still operates on cwd. The `-R` flag (per
  done #5) lets a por or single-repo path be supplied
  directly (sync.rs:160-169). The privileging that
  remains: the `--scope=bot` path errors out for por
  (common.rs:638-647), as it should, but the *default*
  reads the workspace marker — a por workspace silently
  falls through to "current directory only".

Found a **third spot** the review missed. Lines 985–995 — the matrix's "Topology column quick reference":

```
- *Runtime override* (other subcommands): `--por` at
  invocation short-circuits `default_scope` →
  `Scope([Code])` regardless of workspace contents.
  Default-dual still applies at the workspace level
  (the `.vc-config.toml` says dual, but the user is
  asking for "this command only, just my code").
```

Same misread — explicitly says "just my code." Proposed rewrite:

```
- *Runtime override* (other subcommands): `--por`
  treats the target repo (`.` by default, or
  `-R/--repo <path>`) as single — no sibling
  traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo` lookup.
  Symmetric across code and bot. Default-dual at the
  workspace level still applies (`.vc-config.toml`
  still says dual); `--por` is a per-invocation
  override asking "treat the target as single for
  this command."
```

**False positives I checked and skipped** (all describe correct behavior, not the misread):
- `:168`, `:272`, `:318` — `default_scope` returning `Scope([Code])` when there's no workspace root or no `other-repo` key. That's correct in a por workspace (no `.claude/` exists, so scope IS just `Code`). Describes mechanism, not `--por` semantics.
- `:173` — "a por run still operates on cwd" — describes today's sync behavior; cwd IS the target in a por workspace. Consistent with target-relative framing.
- `:1045` — broken-dual fallback to `Scope([Code])` is described as a footgun and is the subject of Gap #7's fix. Already flagged for change.

**Copying.md is clean** — no asymmetric phrasing.

Want me to apply all three rewrites (Spot 1, 2, 3) now?

toolUseResult.newString:
- **Runtime `--por`** — overrides workspace topology
  detection across every subcommand (not just
  `init`/`clone`). Treats the target repo (`.` by
  default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single: no sibling
  traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo` lookup, no
  cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric across code
  and bot:
  - `--por -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate on
    code, no sibling.
  - `--por -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`, no
    sibling.
  - `--por -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate on that
    repo as-is.

content.text:
Concern #1 marked Applied. Disposition table updated.

Open items: #2 Privacy half, #3 (`--init-from-recursive`), #4 (`--repo none` × Topology), #5 (list-valued CLI-vs-config), #6 (gap-list ordering), N1–N4, Pr.

Which next?

message.content: Maybe rather than deleting in max-review-1 maybe we should revert the change and then commit it so we can see the changes overtime?

content.text:
Good call. That matches the project's "git is the record" pattern — the critique snapshot in `max-review-1.md` is the narrative as-captured; commits show what was applied and when. Editing the Status fields turns the snapshot into a mutating tracker and overwrites the original critique reasoning.

**Suggest:** revert all my `max-review-1.md` edits (both per-item Status bodies and the Disposition table rows). Track open vs. applied via either:
- The existing Ideas #2 entry in `todo.md` (already the "resume by walking items" pointer); or
- A new chores section once a cycle opens for this work, with `Commits:` linking back.

One thing to settle first, though — the **commit shape** is the Ideas #2 open question we haven't answered yet:

- **Amend `c0f3376f`** (still unpushed, so amendable via `jj edit -r c0f3376f`). Keeps 0.61.0 as a single coherent close-out commit including the review-driven fixes. Critique-as-applied folds into the doc that's being shipped.
- **New cycle (`0.62.0-0` Preparation)** — the review edits land as `0.62.0-1`, `-2`, etc., with their own commit titles. Cleaner history if more items get applied; also lets the cycle pick up other things (T7, etc.) in the same arc.

Ideas #2 noted you were leaning new-cycle "if non-trivial." Three Concern #1 spots + Concern #2 Topology is already non-trivial. I'd lean new cycle too.

Want me to revert `max-review-1.md` now, and pause for the commit-shape decision?

message.content: We don't need to make a formal set of git changes I suggest reverting, "commiting" that file then a `jj new` and then all subequent change can be deletes. Maybe I'm talking gibberish

content.text:
Not gibberish — that's a clean snapshot pattern. Let me sketch what I think you mean and confirm before doing it.

Current `@` (uncommitted) holds three things:
1. `max-review-1.md` — with my Status/Disposition edits I'd revert.
2. `por-dual-parity-audit.md` — the 4 applied rewrites (Concern #1 ×3 spots, Concern #2 Topology).
3. `todo.md` — the Ideas #2 entry.

Two ways to read "commit that file":

**A. Single mixed commit (simpler).** Revert (1) to pristine, then `jj commit` capturing all three together (pristine critique + applied changes + ideas), then `jj new`. One snapshot. The critique-as-written is preserved at that commit; future deletions in `max-review-1.md` diff against it.

**B. Split: max-review-1.md alone (cleaner separation).** Revert (1), then `jj split` to put pristine `max-review-1.md` in its own commit and the audit/todo changes in another, then `jj new`. The captured critique sits alone in a commit titled "snapshot: max review #1 as captured" or similar; the applications are a separate sibling commit.

A few notes either way:

- **Reverting (1) — no git history to restore from.** `max-review-1.md` is `A` in git status (never committed), so I'd reverse my 4 edits manually rather than `jj restore`. I have the diffs in this session's record.
- **This sits outside cycle protocol.** You said "no formal set of git changes" — so neither A nor B follows the cycle ladder (`X.Y.Z-N` titles, per-commit `cargo fmt`/`clippy`/`test`/`install`). For docs-only edits the install step is skip-able per protocol, so the cost is mainly the unconventional title. We'd plan to fold this into a proper cycle later (likely 0.62.0).
- **`jj new` after either** creates an empty `@` on top; future per-item resolutions (delete applied sections from `max-review-1.md`, plus audit-doc edits, plus todo.md updates) accumulate there.

I lean **A** — simpler, fewer steps, and a single snapshot commit is the lowest overhead given this is informal. **B** earns its complexity only if we want the pristine capture isolated from any applied changes, which we don't really need (git can show `max-review-1.md` at any commit).

Which one? Or am I still reading it wrong?

lastPrompt: We don't need to make a formal set of git changes I suggest reverting, "commiting" that file then a `jj new` and then all subequent change can be deletes. Maybe I'm talking gibberish

message.content: A

content.content:
277	### Shape at close-out push
278	
279	Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing —
280	the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface
281	the options, wait for the user.
282	
283	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
284	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
285	  focused on one or two files.
286	- **Merge non-ff** — `main` gains a merge commit
287	  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via two
288	  parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
289	  shape. Set up: `jj rebase -r <tip> --onto <prev>
290	  --onto <sub-tip>` where `<prev>` is the previous
291	  cycle's closed-out commit (current main tip). Two
292	  `--onto` → `<tip>` becomes a merge of `<prev>` +
293	  `<sub-tip>`.
294	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
295	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
296	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
297	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
298	
299	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
300	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
301	
302	### vc-x1 push wrapper
303	
304	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
305	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.
306	
307	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
308	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
309	shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
310	planned:
311	
312	- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
313	- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
314	- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).
315	
316	### .claude cadence
317	
318	**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
319	with every code commit in that push.
320	
321	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
322	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
323	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
324	code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.
325	
326	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
327	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
328	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
329	app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
330	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
331	
332	### Bot communication at the reviews
333	
334	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
335	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
336	
337	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
338	  "Work complete. Please review."
339	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
340	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
341	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
342	  --body ...` invocation by default.
343	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
344	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
345	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
346	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
347	
348	### After finalize: stop and wait
349	
350	After `vc-x1 finalize` launches (mid-session per-push or
351	at session-end), you **MUST NEVER** proceed (next step,
352	edit, tool call, text output) until the user explicitly
353	directs you to continue. **Even when the next step seems
354	obvious — wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for the
355	whole turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
356	*before* executing finalize. Auto-proceeding bypasses
357	the push+finalize checkpoint the user controls.
358	
359	### Recovery
360	
361	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
362	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
363	  finalize by hand:
364	
365	  ```
366	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log

file.content:
### Shape at close-out push

Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing —
the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface
the options, wait for the user.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** — `main` gains a merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via two
  parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. Set up: `jj rebase -r <tip> --onto <prev>
  --onto <sub-tip>` where `<prev>` is the previous
  cycle's closed-out commit (current main tip). Two
  `--onto` → `<tip>` becomes a merge of `<prev>` +
  `<sub-tip>`.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
planned:

- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
with every code commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

### After finalize: stop and wait

After `vc-x1 finalize` launches (mid-session per-push or
at session-end), you **MUST NEVER** proceed (next step,
edit, tool call, text output) until the user explicitly
directs you to continue. **Even when the next step seems
obvious — wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for the
whole turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
*before* executing finalize. Auto-proceeding bypasses
the push+finalize checkpoint the user controls.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log

lastPrompt: Maybe we'd be smart to push 0.61.0 so we can recover if a catastrophe. Let's push a Merge non-ff change at 0.61.0 and then do our rework on top just to be safe. Is that "easy"?

content.text:
Honest assessment: **doable, but not "1-command easy"** given our current state.

**State**:
- 10 unpushed code commits (0.61.0-0..-7 + close-out `c0f3376f` + snapshot `604fb9e8`).
- All 0.61.0 cycle commits share one ochid → `.claude` change `yvqzvvvuyvqv` (one session for the whole cycle).
- `.claude` `yvqzvvvuyvqv` has no description set — it's been a mutable `@` the whole cycle.
- Snapshot commit `604fb9e8` has **no ochid trailer** (informally committed via raw `jj` rather than `vc-x1 push`).

**Gotchas for merge non-ff**:
1. **`vc-x1 push` wrapper doesn't fully support merge non-ff** per `cycle-protocol.md > vc-x1 push wrapper` — manual `jj git push` needed for the close-out push.
2. **`.claude` side needs the `yvqzvvvuyvqv` change described** before push (it's been an unnamed running session).
3. **Snapshot `604fb9e8` lives above c0f3376f** — jj should auto-rebase it on top of the new merge (it stays local since `main` will only point at the merge). Good.
4. **Bookmark `main`**: at `41ef884` (0.60.0); needs to move to the new merge commit.

**Recipe sketch** (5 steps, all reversible until the `git push`):

```
# 1. Rewrite c0f3376f as a merge of 41ef884 + 587749d
jj rebase -r worlryqm --onto 41ef884 --onto 587749d -R .

# 2. Point app main at the new merge
jj bookmark set main -r worlryqm -R .

# 3. Describe the .claude session for 0.61.0
jj describe -r yvqzvvvuyvqv -m "..." -m "..." -R .claude

# 4. Point .claude main at the finalized session
jj bookmark set main -r yvqzvvvuyvqv -R .claude

# 5. Push both sides
jj git push --bookmark main -R .
jj git push --bookmark main -R .claude
```

The .claude description (step 3) is the trickiest part — it should be one message describing the whole cycle session (the bot has been driving 0.61.0 across many code commits but `.claude` is just one change).

**Easier alternative**: keep-separate push via the wrapper (`vc-x1 push`). Gets the same recovery point, locks in linear public history instead of trapezoidal. Mechanically much simpler since the wrapper handles bookmark/finalize/pairing.

Want to proceed with merge non-ff, or switch to keep-separate for the recovery push?

message.content: I think you mixed sha1 with chid, please use jj chid's

content.text:
You're right — sorry, mixed SHAs with change IDs. Corrected recipe (chid's throughout):

```
# 1. Rewrite worlryqm (0.61.0 close-out) as a merge of
#    nxnorwwn (0.60.0) + wmmkrtpl (0.61.0-7)
jj rebase -r worlryqm --onto nxnorwwn --onto wmmkrtpl -R .

# 2. Point app main at worlryqm (now the merge)
jj bookmark set main -r worlryqm -R .

# 3. Describe the .claude session change
jj describe -r yvqzvvvuyvqv -m "title" -m "body" -R .claude

# 4. Point .claude main at yvqzvvvuyvqv
jj bookmark set main -r yvqzvvvuyvqv -R .claude

# 5. Push both sides
jj git push --bookmark main -R .
jj git push --bookmark main -R .claude
```

Change IDs in play:
- `worlryqm` → 0.61.0 close-out (currently `c0f3376f`)
- `wmmkrtpl` → 0.61.0-7 sub-tip (currently `587749d`)
- `nxnorwwn` → 0.60.0 prev (currently `41ef884`)
- `yvqzvvvuyvqv` → .claude running session
- `opmqmusx` → the snapshot (`604fb9e8`); stays local on top of the new merge

Want to proceed with merge non-ff, or switch to keep-separate?

1	# por/dual parity audit (0.61.0-1)
2	
3	Read-only snapshot audit of every place the `dual` workspace
4	topology (code repo + `.claude/` companion, cross-linked by
5	`ochid:` trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) gets a
6	privileged code path over `por` (Plain Old Repo — single code
7	repo, no `.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`). Snapshot taken at
8	commit `463e6bd9` (`docs: prep por/dual parity audit
9	(0.61.0-0)`). Parent design item is T5 in `todo.md` [[1]];
10	design stub is `por-dual-parity.md` [[2]].
11	
12	- Scope: every subcommand, the `--por` flag's reach, the
13	  ochid-touching helpers, test fixtures.
14	- Out of scope: proposing equalization shape (each finding
15	  carries a one-sentence sketch only — concrete fixes are
16	  follow-up cycles).
17	- Method: grep + read-through. No code changes.
18	
19	## Status
20	
21	**Pre-implementation design snapshot.** This document
22	captures the audit findings (`## 1`–`## 8`), the
23	commonality analysis (`## Commonality`), and the
24	negotiated design (`## Feature axes`) at the end of cycle
25	0.61.0. The matrix, the per-axis Decisions blocks, and
26	the resolution chain are the *current target*, not the
27	final shape.
28	
29	**The implementation will diverge from this.** Every
30	design stub does — implementation surfaces edge cases,
31	forces compromises, and reveals constraints the design
32	pass didn't anticipate. Treat what's here as the starting
33	position for 0.62.0+ equalization cycles, not a contract
34	the code must conform to. When implementation finds the
35	design wrong, update the design (or annotate the
36	divergence in chores) — don't bend code to fit a stale
37	sketch.
38	
39	## 1. `options_flags/por.rs` — the `--por` gate
40	
41	- Files touched:
42	  - `src/options_flags/por.rs` (whole file, 1-20).
43	  - Consumers: `src/init.rs:13,61-65,592-596,1199-1203`,
44	    `src/clone.rs:26,48-51,70,81,143-163`,
45	    `src/init/params.rs:39,60`.
46	  - Test references only:
47	    `src/test_helpers.rs:24,105,196`,
48	    `src/init/tests.rs` (many).
49	- Divergence: `PorFlag` is consumed by **exactly two**
50	  subcommands — `init` and `clone`. No other subcommand
51	  surfaces `--por` (`grep "PorFlag" src/` shows zero other
52	  hits). Doc on the flag itself is creation-time framed: "at
53	  creation time" — there is no equivalent runtime flag for
54	  the post-creation commands (`push`, `sync`, `finalize`,
55	  `validate-desc`, `fix-desc`, `show`, etc.) to declare
56	  "treat this workspace as por".
57	- Severity / category: *Defaulting* + *Feature gap*. Absent
58	  flag = dual; `--por` is a creation-only override; runtime
59	  commands infer topology indirectly (presence /
60	  `workspace.other-repo` value of `.vc-config.toml`).
61	- Equalization sketch: The bot thinks the path is to either
62	  promote `PorFlag` into a shared option flag plumbed
63	  through every command, or commit to topology-from-config
64	  uniformly (no `--por` runtime flag, all commands read
65	  `default_scope` / `.vc-config.toml` and branch from
66	  there). The latter is closer to today's `sync`-style
67	  resolution.
68	
69	## 2. `init` — dual privileged at the orchestration layer
70	
71	- Files touched:
72	  - `src/init.rs` — `plan_init` (583-641), `create_por`
73	    (1217-1265), `create_dual` (1280-1380),
74	    `write_por_vc_config` / `write_por_gitignore` (355-363),
75	    `write_code_config` / `write_session_config` (379-391),
76	    dispatch (1199-1203).
77	  - `src/init/params.rs:39,60` — `por: bool` field.
78	- Divergence: Symmetric on the surface (`create_por` /
79	  `create_dual` are sibling orchestrators), but the
80	  defaulting is asymmetric:
81	  - `plan_init` synthesizes `Scope(vec![Side::Code])` when
82	    `params.por` else `Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot])`
83	    (init.rs:592-596).
84	  - `--config` is rejected unless `--por` is set
85	    (init.rs:608-613, "`--config` is only valid with
86	    `--por` (dual configs are per-side and unconditional)")

**Phase A status**: The rebase succeeded but surfaced a conflict on `opmqmusx` (the snapshot) in `notes/todo.md`. Inspection:

- `worlryqm` (the new merge) is **clean**, parents `nxnorwwn` + `wmmkrtpl` as expected.
- `opmqmusx` (snapshot) is **in conflict** because it modified `notes/todo.md` (the Ideas #2 entry); the rebase reparenting changed the diff baseline.
- `@` (empty) inherits the conflict.

The merge commit itself is clean — the conflict is local to our snapshot, which stays local. Two options:

1. **Resolve the conflict** in `opmqmusx`'s `notes/todo.md` now, so the snapshot stays usable on top of the new merge.
2. **`jj undo`** the rebase, set the snapshot aside (e.g., abandon temporarily), redo the rebase clean, then restore the snapshot's content after.

Option 1 is simpler. Want me to inspect the conflict markers and resolve?

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
  `Commits:` ref** — see
  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
  Move into `## In Progress` as:
  - A **bold title line** — exact match of the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**.
  - A **plan ladder**.

  See [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form). A Todo that
  duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
  bug.
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
  close-out title.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
implement the change. As many as needed:

- Each commit runs through the
  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
  visibility).
- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
  [Pushing](#pushing)).
- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
  scope grows enough (see
  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Ideas

 Items not yet solid enough for `## Todo` (or surfaced
 during close-out / end-of-day before they're fully
 formed). Triaged at the next Preparation: promote to
 `## Todo` / `notes/todo-backlog.md`, fold into a
 picked-up cycle, or drop.

1. **Codify ochid invariant + bot-repo rules + squash
   gating + cross-repo migration in
   `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.** Was planned for
   `0.59.0-2` but deferred when 0.59.0 closed out via
   squash + Option F (manual bot-side rewrite). The
   rules were exercised manually for the close-out;
   this Idea formally codifies them.
   - Codify the **ochid invariant** in `## ochid
     trailers`: every public ochid must resolve in the
     public graph.
   - Codify bot-repo rules: never squashed; descriptions
     editable (`jj describe` preserves chid).
   - Codify squash gating: until `vc-x1 push --squash`
     exists, manual symmetric squash (Option F: app
     squash + bot-side trailer rewrite + force-push) is
     the standard recipe; merge non-ff is the default
     shape for multi-commit cycles.
   - Sketch cross-repo migration: ochids change at
     every merge until the change reaches the canonical
     repo's `main`.
   - Apply Ideas-aware Preparation/Close-out updates:
     Preparation triages `## Ideas` (promote / fold /
     drop) before declaring the plan; Close-out
     captures unresolved follow-ups into `## Ideas`.

## Priorities

- P1 is highest priority, same priority are grouped equally
- A line with `...` is a prefix-match against a `## Todo` title
  - resolves locally (in this file); should be unique
- Entries without a prefix-match are free-form
  - chores tasks, ad-hoc reminders.

### P1

- `**vc-x1 push: support...**`
- `**vc-x1 push --squash...**`

### P2

- `**single-field...**`

### P3

- `**por -> dual...**`

## Todo

 Prioritized entries (referenced by `## Priorities`) lower priority
 todo's are in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). In all cases
 we use the [Prose Form in CLAUDE.md](/CLAUDE.md#prose-form). When
 more detail is waranted those reside in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`.
 Also, we use the  design subsections (link via `[N]` ref). Run
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.

1. **vc-x1 push: support new cycle protocol shape (N:1 code↔bot).**
   Today push assumes 1:1 symmetric WC commits with shared
   title/body. The new cycle protocol has a different shape on
   each side:
   - code side: fully committed before push (N commits via
     merge), nothing to commit at push time
   - `.claude` side: one commit with its own message (distinct
     from any code commit) and a multi-line `ochid:` listing
     all N code commits
