184	## docs: por/dual parity design (0.61.0)
185	
186	Commits: TBD (backfills at next cycle's Preparation)
187	
188	Design cycle for T5 (`por/dual parity + dual → por`).
189	Scope grew over eight commits from an audit to a full
190	CLI design — the audit doc became the canonical record
191	for both the parity divergences (where dual gets
192	privileged code paths over por) and the negotiated
193	designed surface (feature axes, decisions per axis,
194	resolution chain with baked-in default, subcommand ×
195	parameter matrix, copying mechanism for file seeding).
196	Output is input for 0.62.0+ equalization cycles. No
197	code changes land in 0.61.0 itself.
198	
199	The cycle started as "audit + ranked Todos for future
200	fixes" and grew when the audit's findings made clear
201	that defaulting topology (the user's original framing)
202	wouldn't be safe before equalizing the underlying paths,
203	and that `--por` itself was a tangled bundle of
204	independent feature axes (topology, `.vc-config.toml`
205	write, remote, privacy, copying, scaffolding) rather
206	than a single toggle. The In Progress block (moved
207	below) records the problem statement and the as-built
208	ladder.
209	
210	### Problem statement (preserved from In Progress)
211	
212	Audit where `dual` (code + `.claude/` companion, cross-
213	linked by `ochid:`) gets privileged code paths vs `por`
214	(plain single repo). Goal: produce a parity-gap document
215	that drives future parity-equalization cycles. T5 in
216	`## Todo` is the parent design item; this cycle is the
217	audit step that precedes any code-level equalization.
218	
219	The user proposed starting from `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
220	toml` to make the topology choice user-configurable; the
221	bot counter-proposed that a defaults knob shouldn't ship
222	before the underlying paths are equalized, and the user
223	agreed — audit first, equalize next, then expose

file.content:
## docs: por/dual parity design (0.61.0)

Commits: TBD (backfills at next cycle's Preparation)

Design cycle for T5 (`por/dual parity + dual → por`).
Scope grew over eight commits from an audit to a full
CLI design — the audit doc became the canonical record
for both the parity divergences (where dual gets
privileged code paths over por) and the negotiated
designed surface (feature axes, decisions per axis,
resolution chain with baked-in default, subcommand ×
parameter matrix, copying mechanism for file seeding).
Output is input for 0.62.0+ equalization cycles. No
code changes land in 0.61.0 itself.

The cycle started as "audit + ranked Todos for future
fixes" and grew when the audit's findings made clear
that defaulting topology (the user's original framing)
wouldn't be safe before equalizing the underlying paths,
and that `--por` itself was a tangled bundle of
independent feature axes (topology, `.vc-config.toml`
write, remote, privacy, copying, scaffolding) rather
than a single toggle. The In Progress block (moved
below) records the problem statement and the as-built
ladder.

### Problem statement (preserved from In Progress)

Audit where `dual` (code + `.claude/` companion, cross-
linked by `ochid:`) gets privileged code paths vs `por`
(plain single repo). Goal: produce a parity-gap document
that drives future parity-equalization cycles. T5 in
`## Todo` is the parent design item; this cycle is the
audit step that precedes any code-level equalization.

The user proposed starting from `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` to make the topology choice user-configurable; the
bot counter-proposed that a defaults knob shouldn't ship
before the underlying paths are equalized, and the user
agreed — audit first, equalize next, then expose

content.text:
Sound idea — each Concern as a -N change gives clean per-item granularity. Two clarifications before we shape Concern #1's bookkeeping:

**1. Does "move #1 to chores-12" mean delete from `max-review-1.md`, or just add a narrative bullet to chores-12?**

Earlier we restored `max-review-1.md` to pristine (after you proposed "revert change, commit so we can see changes over time"). Deleting Concern #1's section from it now would contradict that — `max-review-1.md` would shrink as items are applied, with git holding the original capture.

The alternative reading: keep `max-review-1.md` pristine, just **add** a narrative bullet to `chores-12.md > 0.62.0` recording what 0.62.0-1 accomplished.

**2. Done entry per Concern, or per cycle?**

The project convention so far is one Done entry per cycle, added at close-out (with a `[N]` ref to the chores section). The 0.61.0 close-out body even calls it out: "new ## Done entry **for the cycle**". A Done entry per Concern would diverge — possible (Done is just a rolling list), but it'd be unusual.

**3. Retroactive for Concern #1 specifically:**

0.62.0-1 is committed (unpushed) but it only did the audit-doc work, not the bookkeeping you're describing. Three ways to add bookkeeping:

- (a) **Amend 0.62.0-1** — it's still unpushed and mutable via `jj edit dbae713e`. Single coherent "do + record" commit.
- (b) **Add 0.62.0-2 as a bookkeeping commit** — separates work from record.
- (c) **Fold into the next Concern's commit** — bundle Concern #1's bookkeeping with the next item's work.

Which combination do you want?

message.content: 1. My feeling max-review is a Todo list and they should be moved (aka deleted from max-review, like we do with todo).

content.text:
Got it — `max-review-1.md` is the cycle's todo list; applied items get **deleted** from it, and git history is the "over time" record (each deletion is a visible commit). That reconciles with your earlier "commit so we can see changes over time" — the commits *are* the record.

This means the clean shape is: **`-1` seeds the full list; each later `-N` applies one item AND deletes it from the list.** But `0.62.0-1` currently bundles *seed full list* + *apply #1/#2T audit edits* — without the deletion. Two ways to fix:

- **A. Re-split `0.62.0-1`** (it's unpushed, cheap to redo via `jj edit dbae713e`):
  - `0.62.0-1` → seed the full `max-review-1.md` only (revert the audit edits).
  - `0.62.0-2` → apply Concern #1 (3 audit spots) + delete #1 from the list + chores bullet.
  - `0.62.0-3` → apply Concern #2 Topology + trim #2 to Privacy-only + chores bullet.
  - Matches your model exactly; git shows full list → minus #1 → #2 trimmed.
- **B. Catch-up** — keep `0.62.0-1` as-is, add `0.62.0-2` that retires #1 + #2T from the list + chores bullet. Less surgery, but `-1`'s audit edits and their list-retirement live in different commits.

**Two wrinkles either way:**

1. **Concern #2 is half-done** — Topology applied, Privacy still open. It can't be fully deleted; it gets *trimmed* to a Privacy-only entry.
2. **Done entry** — I'd keep the convention of one Done entry at cycle close-out (with a `[N]` ref to the chores-12 section), and let chores-12 carry the per-item narrative. Per-Concern Done entries are possible but diverge.

I lean **A** — it's only cheap right now because `0.62.0-1` is unpushed, and it makes every later commit follow one clean rule. Which do you want?

**Decisions (0.61.0):**

- **Add `--dual` peer flag**. `--por` / `--dual`
  exactly-one-of (clap-enforced). Internal value is an
  enum, not a boolean — eliminates the `--por`/`!--por`
  framing that the audit found everywhere.
- **Default: `dual`** (user reversed from an earlier
  strict-explicit-required position once the broader
  "defaults where natural" principle landed). Dual is
  the primary use case; defaulting to it matches
  reality and minimizes new-user friction.
- **Runtime override on every subcommand** (not just
  `init` / `clone`). `--por` at runtime short-circuits
  `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` regardless of what
  the workspace says. Escape hatch for "this command,
  just my code, leave `.claude/` alone."
- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml` is dual-only**. Por
  never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` gets
  dropped.

## Concerns

### 1. Runtime `--por` semantics — doc text vs. intent

**Original critique** (max, 2026-05-26): The Topology
Decisions block at `audit.md:530-534` reads:

> `--por` at runtime short-circuits `default_scope` →
> **`Scope([Code])`** regardless of what the workspace
> says. Escape hatch for "this command, just my code,
> leave `.claude/` alone."

The bot read that as code-privileged: creation-time
`--por` means "this workspace is single," runtime `--por`
means "ignore the bot side this invocation." Two
different operations mounted on one spelling — felt
overloaded.

**User clarification** (wink, 2026-05-26): runtime `--por`
isn't side-specific. It means **"treat the repo I'm
pointed at (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as a
single repo — no sibling lookup."** Symmetric across
code and bot:

- `--por -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate on code,
  no sibling.
- `--por -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`, no sibling.
- `--por -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate on that
  repo as-is.

Creation-time `--por` is a special case of the same rule
(the target doesn't exist yet, so "treat as standalone"
collapses to "create as standalone"). One rule, two life
stages.

**Bot retraction:** the "two meanings on one spelling"
critique evaporates under this framing. Also: the bot's
proposed alternative ("use `--scope code` instead") was
wrong — it conflates *selecting one side of a known dual
workspace* (`--scope`) with *declaring the target isn't
dual* (`--por`). Different mental models, not
interchangeable.

**Status:** **resolved on framing; doc-text update
pending.** The audit doc currently codifies the narrower
code-asymmetric reading at two spots:

- `audit.md:530-534` (Topology Decisions, "runtime
  override" bullet) — says `→ Scope([Code])`, "just my
  code."
- `audit.md:897-913` (Por's view of the chain, "Runtime
  `--por`" bullet) — says "short-circuits
  `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` and ignores
  `.claude/`."

**Action:** at the next pickup of this design (likely
0.62.0 Preparation), rewrite both spots to spell out the
target-relative semantics. Suggested text for the
Topology bullet:

> Runtime override on every subcommand: `--por` declares
> the target repo (default `.`, or `-R/--repo <path>`)
> as single — no sibling traversal, no `.vc-config.toml
> > other-repo` lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation.
> Symmetric across code and bot — `--por` doesn't
> privilege a side, it suppresses the sibling-discovery
> step.

### 2. "Exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" is contradictory phrasing

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Max review #1 — 0.61.0 por/dual parity design

Captured 2026-05-26 (max effort) after the close-out commit
of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
disposition), or applied (already changed).

## Scope reviewed

- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).
- [`copying.md`](copying.md) — copying mechanism design
  stub from `0.61.0-4`.
- [`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md) — older
  forward-looking doc (read for context only; not
  critiqued).
- [`/notes/chores/chores-12.md > ## docs: por/dual parity
  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
  cycle narrative.

Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
durable references.

## Strengths

For balance — what the bot thinks works well:

- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality
  inversion → axis decomposition). The `-2` inversion is
  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling
  out `--por` as a bundle of six axes opens design space
  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
  a real defect of the bundle framing.
- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
  initially listed as independent; the design pass
  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
  elegantly.
- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
  concrete equalization that validates the
  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
  itself for in 0.65.0.

## Concerns

### 2. "Exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" is contradictory phrasing

**Critique:** `audit.md:521-522` says clap-enforced
exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual`; `:525` says
default-dual. If neither flag is passed, either clap
rejects (true exactly-one-of, friction for the common
case) or it accepts (default kicks in, "exactly-one-of"
is misleading). Same shape on `--private` / `--public`
at `:643-647`.

**Status:** open.

**Proposed action:** spell it "**at most one of**;
absence → workspace-config or baked-in default." Honest
about what the default does. Same fix for Privacy. Docs-
only edit, no code implications.

### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability

**Critique:** `copying.md:29-38` defines six flags:
`--init-from`, `--init-from-code`, `--init-from-bot`,
and `-recursive` variants of each. But recursion is a
property of the *operand*, not the flag: `cp src/* dst/`
is recursive whether you said `-r` or not; `cp
src/foo.txt dst/` isn't. The shell expands the glob; the
bot thinks vc-x1 should walk each resolved path and do
the right thing.

**Proposed action:** one flag per side.
`--init-from-code=<glob>` does the right thing per
resolved path (file → file copy; dir → recursive copy).
Cuts the surface in half, matches `cp` semantics users
already have in their head. The `-recursive` naming
muddies what the flag controls vs. what the operand
controls.

**Status:** open. Material to copying.md design before
that cycle starts.

### 4. `--repo none` × Topology interaction isn't spelled out

**Critique:** the Remote axis Decisions
(`audit.md:596-624`) introduces `--repo none` as a peer
category, with `--account` skipped under it. Fine in
isolation. But `--repo none` × `--dual` means "dual
workspace with no remote for either side." The
bot/sync/push workflow assumes remote-backed `.claude/`.
The doc doesn't say whether dual+no-remote is
first-class or rejected at parse time.

**Proposed action:** add one paragraph to the Remote
axis (or matrix footnotes) on the Topology × Remote=none
interaction. Don't leave it implicit. The bot thinks a
future user trying `init --dual --repo none` will hit a
confused error later in the workflow.

**Status:** open. Affects implementation when `--repo
none` lands (Gap #5).

### 5. List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" semantics deserves an explicit callout

**Critique:** `audit.md:697-699` says: "If user passes
any `--init-from*` on CLI, the config-pinned list is
ignored — match the broader chain rule."

The "broader chain rule" is about *scalar* values where
"wins" unambiguously means "replaces." For list-valued
axes, the bot thinks users coming from `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`
/ `PATH` will expect merge, not replace. The replace
choice is defensible (predictability beats expressivity)
but it's not the obvious choice.

**Proposed action:** keep the replace semantics, but add
an explicit "list-typed-axis rule" sentence — "for
list-valued axes like Copying, CLI replaces config; to
merge, pass the config globs on CLI too." Prevents the
same conversation in 0.62.0.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

### 6. Gap-list ordering hides one structural dependency

**Critique:** `audit.md:1010-1080` ranks gaps by "blast
radius." But Gap #7 (`default_scope` broken-dual
detection) is a *prerequisite* for Gap #9 (copying
mechanism), per `copying.md:81-92` — copying defers
validation to the first downstream subcommand, which
means broken-dual detection has to exist first.

**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
"ranking is rough; some structural prerequisites (e.g.
#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
renumbering.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

## Nits

Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
  hardening a use case nobody asked for.

- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.

- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
  get consulted?") — a different angle.

- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Runtime `--por` semantics — doc text vs. intent | Resolved on framing; doc-text update pending | `audit.md` Topology Decisions + "Por's view" subsections |
| 2 | "Exactly-one-of with default" phrasing | Open | `audit.md` Topology + Privacy Decisions |
| 3 | `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface | Open | `copying.md` Surface section |
| 4 | `--repo none` × Topology interaction | Open | `audit.md` Remote Decisions or matrix footnote |
| 5 | List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" | Open | `audit.md` Copying Decisions |
| 6 | Gap-list ordering hides one prereq | Open | `audit.md` Gap list intro |
| N1 | `finalize` matrix row footnote | Open | `audit.md` matrix |
| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | Open | `audit.md` top |
| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | Open | `audit.md` § "Por's view" |
| N4 | `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` topology-blind note | Open | `audit.md` (anywhere appropriate) |
| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |

## TL;DR

Items 1 and 3 are the bot's highest-conviction concerns —
the runtime `--por` semantics doc (now resolved on
framing but doc text still needs the rewrite) and the
`--init-from-recursive` surface doubling. The rest are
docs-only edits that don't block 0.62.0 starting on T7,
but each is cheaper to apply now (single design pass)
than after the next ten cycles have referenced the
current text.

# References

[1]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md

**`0.62.0-2` committed** (`37f98e6a`) — Concern #1 applied:

- audit.md: 3 spots rewritten (target-relative symmetric `--por`).
- max-review-1.md: Concern #1 section + Disposition row deleted (it's the working list).
- chores-12.md: 0.62.0 intro revised + Concern #1 narrative bullet added.

Chain: `0.61.0 merge → -0 prep → -1 seed → -2 Concern #1`. `@` empty above.

**One thing I deferred**: no Done entry yet — I'm holding to one Done entry at cycle close-out (with a `[N]` ref to the chores section), per convention. If you'd rather have a per-Concern Done entry, say so and I'll add one now.

Next is `0.62.0-3` — Concern #2 Topology (same pattern, except #2 gets **trimmed to Privacy-only** in the review list rather than deleted, since its Privacy half is still open). Proceed?

message.content: I suggest instead of "Exactly-one-of" either "Options --por & --dual are mutually exclusive" but actually we should probably have one option with a value --mode=por|dual

content.text:
`--mode=por|dual` (one value-bearing flag) is the cleaner design — it dissolves the contradiction entirely (a single flag can't conflict with itself; absence → the default value), it's extensible if a third topology ever appears, and it matches the audit's already-decided "internal value is an enum, not a boolean." I'd favor it over rewording two flags.

Two things to settle before I touch the doc:

1. **Value names.** The doc's Topology *States* are `single | dual` (audit.md:508), but you wrote `por | dual`. Aligning the flag values to the state names (`--mode=single|dual`) avoids a por/single split in the vocabulary. Counter: "por" is the established term everywhere else in the doc. Also — `--mode` is generic; `--topology=` is more self-describing. So really three sub-choices: flag name (`--mode` vs `--topology`) and value names (`por|dual` vs `single|dual`).

2. **Scope / ripple.** This is bigger than Concern #2's phrasing fix. `--por`/`--dual` runs through the whole audit doc, and **Concern #1 (just applied in 0.62.0-2) uses runtime `--por`** — adopting `--mode` means the runtime override becomes `--mode=por`, which would revise text we just landed. So it's a topology-surface redesign, not a one-spot edit.

How do you want to scope `0.62.0-3`?

- **(a) Minimal** — keep two flags, reword "exactly-one-of" → "mutually exclusive (at most one; absence → `dual`)." Closes Concern #2 as the review framed it; defer `--mode` to its own item.
- **(b) Adopt `--mode=<value>`** — redesign the topology surface doc-wide, revisit Concern #1's runtime text for consistency. Lands the design you want, larger change.

I lean **(b)** since it's the design you actually want and the doc is still a pre-implementation sketch (cheap to change now) — but it's worth doing as its own tracked decision rather than smuggling it into "Concern #2 phrasing." Which way, and what flag/value names?

lastPrompt: I suggest instead of "Exactly-one-of" either "Options --por & --dual are mutually exclusive" but actually we should probably have one option with a value --mode=por|dual

message.content: (b) --mode=<value> but not sure about single vs por, I guess I lean toward single, although a short value of 1 and 2 for single dual?
(b) --mode=<value> but not sure about single vs por, I guess I lean toward single, and then short value single=1, dual=2?

content.text:
`single` over `por` — agreed. It matches the Topology *State* names (audit.md:508 is already `single | dual`), and it's self-documenting where `por` needs you to know the acronym. "por"/Plain Old Repo can stay as the internal/conceptual term (and the existing `PorFlag`/`FixturePor` code names); the doc should just note `--mode=single` ≡ por so the mapping is explicit.

On `single=1, dual=2` — **I'd push back.** The whole reason `--mode=<value>` beats the two-flag mess is that the value is self-describing; `--mode=2` throws that away and makes the reader memorize an arbitrary map (nothing makes single "1" and dual "2" — topology isn't an ordered scale like `-O0/-O1/-O2`). It also adds parsing surface (clap derives enums from names; numbers need custom aliases). If the goal is fewer keystrokes, better options:

- **Prefix/short aliases**: `--mode=s` / `--mode=d` (clap can accept these as enum aliases) — still mnemonic, unlike digits.
- **Keep `--por` / `--dual` as boolean shortcuts** that desugar to `--mode=single|dual` — terse *and* back-compatible with today's `--por`.

So my recommendation for the Topology surface:

- `--mode=<single|dual>`, default `dual` (absence → dual via the chain).
- Runtime `--mode=single` overrides detected topology for one invocation (restates Concern #1's target-relative rule).
- `single` ≡ por (internal term); doc notes the mapping.
- Optionally `s`/`d` aliases for brevity — *not* numbers.

One last call: flag name `--mode` vs `--topology`? `--mode` is shorter; `--topology` says what it controls. You've said `--mode` consistently, so I'll go with that unless you want the more explicit name.

Want me to write `0.62.0-3` with that shape (and `s`/`d` aliases, yes/no)?

message.content: go

content.content:
519	**Decisions (0.61.0):**
520	
521	- **Add `--dual` peer flag**. `--por` / `--dual`
522	  exactly-one-of (clap-enforced). Internal value is an
523	  enum, not a boolean — eliminates the `--por`/`!--por`
524	  framing that the audit found everywhere.
525	- **Default: `dual`** (user reversed from an earlier
526	  strict-explicit-required position once the broader
527	  "defaults where natural" principle landed). Dual is
528	  the primary use case; defaulting to it matches
529	  reality and minimizes new-user friction.
530	- **Runtime override on every subcommand** (not just
531	  `init` / `clone`). `--por` declares the target repo
532	  (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single —
533	  no sibling traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo`
534	  lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric
535	  across code and bot — `--por` suppresses sibling
536	  discovery for whichever repo is the target, it
537	  doesn't privilege a side.
538	- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml` is dual-only**. Por
539	  never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
540	  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` gets
541	  dropped.
542	- **Resolution chain**: CLI > ENV > Local > Global >
543	  baked-in default (`dual`). No "Error" floor for
544	  Topology — baked-in always supplies.

file.content:
**Decisions (0.61.0):**

- **Add `--dual` peer flag**. `--por` / `--dual`
  exactly-one-of (clap-enforced). Internal value is an
  enum, not a boolean — eliminates the `--por`/`!--por`
  framing that the audit found everywhere.
- **Default: `dual`** (user reversed from an earlier
  strict-explicit-required position once the broader
  "defaults where natural" principle landed). Dual is
  the primary use case; defaulting to it matches
  reality and minimizes new-user friction.
- **Runtime override on every subcommand** (not just
  `init` / `clone`). `--por` declares the target repo
  (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single —
  no sibling traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo`
  lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric
  across code and bot — `--por` suppresses sibling
  discovery for whichever repo is the target, it
  doesn't privilege a side.
- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml` is dual-only**. Por
  never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` gets
  dropped.
- **Resolution chain**: CLI > ENV > Local > Global >
  baked-in default (`dual`). No "Error" floor for
  Topology — baked-in always supplies.

content.text: Rewriting the core Topology Decisions block to the `--mode` design (this also folds Concern #1's runtime semantics into `--mode=single`).

input.old_string:
- **Add `--dual` peer flag**. `--por` / `--dual`
  exactly-one-of (clap-enforced). Internal value is an
  enum, not a boolean — eliminates the `--por`/`!--por`
  framing that the audit found everywhere.
- **Default: `dual`** (user reversed from an earlier
  strict-explicit-required position once the broader
  "defaults where natural" principle landed). Dual is
  the primary use case; defaulting to it matches
  reality and minimizes new-user friction.
- **Runtime override on every subcommand** (not just
  `init` / `clone`). `--por` declares the target repo
  (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single —
  no sibling traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo`
  lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric
  across code and bot — `--por` suppresses sibling
  discovery for whichever repo is the target, it
  doesn't privilege a side.
- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml` is dual-only**. Por
  never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` gets
  dropped.
input.new_string:
- **Topology selected by `--mode=<single|dual>`** — one
  value-bearing flag, not two booleans. Replaces the
  `--por` / `--dual` peer-flag pair; a single flag can't
  conflict with itself, so the "exactly-one-of"
  enforcement disappears. Optional `s` / `d` aliases for
  brevity. Internal value is an enum — eliminates the
  `--por`/`!--por` boolean framing the audit found
  everywhere.
  - `single` (≡ por, "Plain Old Repo"): one code repo,
    no `.claude/` companion.
  - `dual`: code repo + `.claude/` companion, cross-
    linked by `ochid:`.
  - "por" stays the internal / conceptual term (and
    today's `PorFlag` / `FixturePor` code names);
    `single` is the user-facing value.
- **Default: `dual`** — absence of `--mode` → `dual` via
  the resolution chain below. Dual is the primary use
  case and the reason vc-x1 exists. (User reversed from
  an earlier strict-explicit-required position once the
  broader "defaults where natural" principle landed.)
- **Runtime override on every subcommand** (not just
  `init` / `clone`). `--mode=single` declares the target
  repo (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single
  — no sibling traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo`
  lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric
  across code and bot — it suppresses sibling discovery
  for whichever repo is the target, it doesn't privilege
  a side. At creation `--mode` sets the persistent
  topology; at runtime it overrides the detected
  topology for that invocation.
- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml` is dual-only**. `single`
  never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write (under `--mode=single`)
  gets dropped.

### Defaults summary

| Axis | Default | Today's flag | Independent today? |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Topology | dual | `--por` (opt-out only) | No (no `--dual` peer) |
| `.vc-config.toml` write | written | `--config <none\|PATH>` | No (rejected when dual) |
| Remote | github-create | `--repo <cat>[=<val>]` | Mostly (`none` missing) |
| Privacy | public | `--private` | Yes |
| Copying | none | `--use-template <C[,B]>` | Yes (Topology-aware) |
| Scaffolding | on | (none) | Not an axis today |

### Mapping `--por` / `--dual` onto axis combinations

Today `--por` and (implicit) `--dual` are *bundle
shorthands*. After axes are independent, they remain as
shorthands for the common combinations:

- `--dual` (or no flag — back-compat) →
  `(Topology=dual, Vc-config=written, Remote=github-
  create, Privacy=public, Copying=none)`.

file.content:
### Mapping `--por` / `--dual` onto axis combinations

Today `--por` and (implicit) `--dual` are *bundle
shorthands*. After axes are independent, they remain as
shorthands for the common combinations:

- `--dual` (or no flag — back-compat) →
  `(Topology=dual, Vc-config=written, Remote=github-
  create, Privacy=public, Copying=none)`.
- `--por` → `(Topology=single, Vc-config=written,
  Remote=github-create, Privacy=public, Copying=none)`.
  Note `Vc-config=written` is what today's `--por`
  actually does, not `not-written` (the `.vc-config.toml`
  is still written, just degenerate).

A user wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't
spell it; tomorrow it would be `--por --config none --no-remote` (or
shorthand `--bare` if that combination is common enough to
deserve one).

### Connections to user-config

Once axes are independent, each maps to a `[default].*`
key in `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`, so the user-config
proposal that opened this cycle lands cleanly:

- `[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`
- `[default].write-vc-config = true | false | "<path>"`
- `[default].remote = "github" | "local" | "none"`
- `[default].private = true | false`

file.content:
#### Por's view of the chain

Following the decisions in this cycle:

- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml`** (layer 3) — dual-only.
  Por never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write under `--por` is residue
  from when `--por` was a half-implemented opt-out and
  should be dropped.
- **User-config `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`**
  (layer 4) — topology-neutral. Both por and dual consult
  it.
- **Runtime `--por`** — overrides workspace topology
  detection across every subcommand (not just
  `init`/`clone`). Treats the target repo (`.` by
  default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single: no sibling
  traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo` lookup, no
  cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric across code
  and bot:
  - `--por -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate on
    code, no sibling.
  - `--por -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`, no
    sibling.
  - `--por -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate on that
    repo as-is.

### Subcommand × parameter matrix

file.content:
**Topology column (`T`) — quick reference:**

- *Creation* (init/clone): `--por` / `--dual` chooses
  the workspace shape that gets written into
  `.vc-config.toml` (dual only — por writes nothing).
- *Runtime override* (other subcommands): `--por`
  treats the target repo (`.` by default, or
  `-R/--repo <path>`) as single — no sibling
  traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo` lookup.
  Symmetric across code and bot. Default-dual at the
  workspace level still applies (`.vc-config.toml`
  still says dual); `--por` is a per-invocation
  override asking "treat the target as single for
  this command."
- `finalize` accepts T as a runtime override and SC for
  per-side selection. Today's only use site is inside
  `push`'s workflow against a specific repo path, but
  the body itself is generic (squash + commit with
  title/body + push) and works on either side
  standalone — `--scope=code` or `--scope=bot`. Use
  case: a user manually squashing a cycle into one
  commit and pushing. `--squash` is logically optional

  cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric across code
  and bot:
  - `--mode=single -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate
    on code, no sibling.
  - `--mode=single -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`,
    no sibling.
  - `--mode=single -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate
    on that repo as-is.

-  (layer 4) — topology-neutral. Both por and dual consult
+  topology detection across every subcommand (not just

### Mapping `--por` / `--dual` onto axis combinations

Today `--por` and (implicit) `--dual` are *bundle
shorthands*. After axes are independent, they remain as
shorthands for the common combinations:

- `--dual` (or no flag — back-compat) →
  `(Topology=dual, Vc-config=written, Remote=github-
  create, Privacy=public, Copying=none)`.
- `--por` → `(Topology=single, Vc-config=written,
  Remote=github-create, Privacy=public, Copying=none)`.
  Note `Vc-config=written` is what today's `--por`
  actually does, not `not-written` (the `.vc-config.toml`
  is still written, just degenerate).

A user wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't
spell it; tomorrow it would be `--por --config none --no-remote` (or
shorthand `--bare` if that combination is common enough to
deserve one).

### 2. "Exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" is contradictory phrasing

**Critique:** `audit.md:521-522` says clap-enforced
exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual`; `:525` says
default-dual. If neither flag is passed, either clap
rejects (true exactly-one-of, friction for the common
case) or it accepts (default kicks in, "exactly-one-of"
is misleading). Same shape on `--private` / `--public`
at `:643-647`.

**Status:** open.

**Proposed action:** spell it "**at most one of**;
absence → workspace-config or baked-in default." Honest
about what the default does. Same fix for Privacy. Docs-
only edit, no code implications.

content.text: Rewriting Concern #2 to Privacy-only (Topology half resolved via the `--mode` redesign).

input.old_string:
### 2. "Exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" is contradictory phrasing

**Critique:** `audit.md:521-522` says clap-enforced
exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual`; `:525` says
default-dual. If neither flag is passed, either clap
rejects (true exactly-one-of, friction for the common
case) or it accepts (default kicks in, "exactly-one-of"
is misleading). Same shape on `--private` / `--public`
at `:643-647`.

**Status:** open.

**Proposed action:** spell it "**at most one of**;
absence → workspace-config or baked-in default." Honest
about what the default does. Same fix for Privacy. Docs-
only edit, no code implications.
input.new_string:
### 2. Privacy `--private` / `--public` — same "exactly-one-of with default" tension

**Topology half — resolved (0.62.0-3).** The original
critique flagged the contradictory "exactly-one-of
`--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" phrasing. Rather
than reword it, `0.62.0-3` replaced the two flags with a
single `--mode=<single|dual>` value flag — a single flag
can't conflict with itself, so the contradiction is gone.

**Privacy half — open.** The Privacy axis carries the
identical shape: `--private` / `--public` exactly-one-of
with a `[default].private` default (audit.md Privacy
Decisions). If neither is passed, either clap rejects
(friction) or the default kicks in ("exactly-one-of" is
misleading).

**Status:** open (Privacy half).

**Proposed action:** mirror the Topology fix — adopt a
single value flag `--visibility=<private|public>`
(default `public`), optionally with `--private` /
`--public` as shortcuts. Resolves the contradiction the
same way `--mode` did for Topology.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Max review #1 — 0.61.0 por/dual parity design

Captured 2026-05-26 (max effort) after the close-out commit
of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
disposition), or applied (already changed).

## Scope reviewed

- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).
- [`copying.md`](copying.md) — copying mechanism design
  stub from `0.61.0-4`.
- [`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md) — older
  forward-looking doc (read for context only; not
  critiqued).
- [`/notes/chores/chores-12.md > ## docs: por/dual parity
  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
  cycle narrative.

Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
durable references.

## Strengths

For balance — what the bot thinks works well:

- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality
  inversion → axis decomposition). The `-2` inversion is
  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling
  out `--por` as a bundle of six axes opens design space
  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
  a real defect of the bundle framing.
- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
  initially listed as independent; the design pass
  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
  elegantly.
- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
  concrete equalization that validates the
  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
  itself for in 0.65.0.

## Concerns

### 2. "Exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" is contradictory phrasing

**Critique:** `audit.md:521-522` says clap-enforced
exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual`; `:525` says
default-dual. If neither flag is passed, either clap
rejects (true exactly-one-of, friction for the common
case) or it accepts (default kicks in, "exactly-one-of"
is misleading). Same shape on `--private` / `--public`
at `:643-647`.

**Status:** open.

**Proposed action:** spell it "**at most one of**;
absence → workspace-config or baked-in default." Honest
about what the default does. Same fix for Privacy. Docs-
only edit, no code implications.

### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability

**Critique:** `copying.md:29-38` defines six flags:
`--init-from`, `--init-from-code`, `--init-from-bot`,
and `-recursive` variants of each. But recursion is a
property of the *operand*, not the flag: `cp src/* dst/`
is recursive whether you said `-r` or not; `cp
src/foo.txt dst/` isn't. The shell expands the glob; the
bot thinks vc-x1 should walk each resolved path and do
the right thing.

**Proposed action:** one flag per side.
`--init-from-code=<glob>` does the right thing per
resolved path (file → file copy; dir → recursive copy).
Cuts the surface in half, matches `cp` semantics users
already have in their head. The `-recursive` naming
muddies what the flag controls vs. what the operand
controls.

**Status:** open. Material to copying.md design before
that cycle starts.

### 4. `--repo none` × Topology interaction isn't spelled out

**Critique:** the Remote axis Decisions
(`audit.md:596-624`) introduces `--repo none` as a peer
category, with `--account` skipped under it. Fine in
isolation. But `--repo none` × `--dual` means "dual
workspace with no remote for either side." The
bot/sync/push workflow assumes remote-backed `.claude/`.
The doc doesn't say whether dual+no-remote is
first-class or rejected at parse time.

**Proposed action:** add one paragraph to the Remote
axis (or matrix footnotes) on the Topology × Remote=none
interaction. Don't leave it implicit. The bot thinks a
future user trying `init --dual --repo none` will hit a
confused error later in the workflow.

**Status:** open. Affects implementation when `--repo
none` lands (Gap #5).

### 5. List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" semantics deserves an explicit callout

**Critique:** `audit.md:697-699` says: "If user passes
any `--init-from*` on CLI, the config-pinned list is
ignored — match the broader chain rule."

The "broader chain rule" is about *scalar* values where
"wins" unambiguously means "replaces." For list-valued
axes, the bot thinks users coming from `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`
/ `PATH` will expect merge, not replace. The replace
choice is defensible (predictability beats expressivity)
but it's not the obvious choice.

**Proposed action:** keep the replace semantics, but add
an explicit "list-typed-axis rule" sentence — "for
list-valued axes like Copying, CLI replaces config; to
merge, pass the config globs on CLI too." Prevents the
same conversation in 0.62.0.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

### 6. Gap-list ordering hides one structural dependency

**Critique:** `audit.md:1010-1080` ranks gaps by "blast
radius." But Gap #7 (`default_scope` broken-dual
detection) is a *prerequisite* for Gap #9 (copying
mechanism), per `copying.md:81-92` — copying defers
validation to the first downstream subcommand, which
means broken-dual detection has to exist first.

**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
"ranking is rough; some structural prerequisites (e.g.
#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
renumbering.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

## Nits

Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
  hardening a use case nobody asked for.

- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.

- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
  get consulted?") — a different angle.

- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2 | "Exactly-one-of with default" phrasing | Open | `audit.md` Topology + Privacy Decisions |
| 3 | `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface | Open | `copying.md` Surface section |
| 4 | `--repo none` × Topology interaction | Open | `audit.md` Remote Decisions or matrix footnote |
| 5 | List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" | Open | `audit.md` Copying Decisions |
| 6 | Gap-list ordering hides one prereq | Open | `audit.md` Gap list intro |

## docs: apply max review #1 (0.62.0)

Commits: TBD (backfills at next cycle's Preparation)

Apply accepted items from
[`max-review-1.md`](../design-cli/max-review-1.md) to
the por/dual parity audit doc. The review file is the
cycle's working list: `0.62.0-1` seeds the full
capture, each later `-N` applies one item and deletes
it from the list (git history holds the original).

- **Concern #1 — runtime `--por` semantics** (0.62.0-2).
  Three doc-text spots (Topology Decisions "Runtime
  override" bullet, "Por's view of the chain" bullet,
  matrix Topology-column sub-bullet) read runtime
  `--por` as code-privileged (`→ Scope([Code])`, "just
  my code"). Rewrote all three to the target-relative
  rule: `--por` declares the target repo (`.` or
  `-R/--repo <path>`) single and suppresses sibling
  discovery symmetrically across code and bot — it
  doesn't privilege a side.

rule: `--por` declares the target repo (`.` or
  `-R/--repo <path>`) single and suppresses sibling
  discovery symmetrically across code and bot — it
  doesn't privilege a side.
- **`--mode=<single|dual>` topology surface** (0.62.0-3).
  Concern #2 flagged the contradictory "exactly-one-of
  `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" phrasing. Rather
  than reword, replaced the two boolean flags with one
  value-bearing flag `--mode=<single|dual>` (default
  `dual`, `single` ≡ por, optional `s` / `d` aliases) —
  a single flag can't conflict with itself, so the
  contradiction dissolves. Propagated across the design
  sections (Topology Decisions, Por's view, matrix +
  quick-reference, Mapping section, Gap #8); the runtime
  override from Concern #1 becomes `--mode=single`. Audit
  findings (`## 1`–`## 8`) keep `--por` — they describe
  today's code. Concern #2's Privacy half (same shape on
  `--private` / `--public`) stays open; a parallel
  `--visibility=<private|public>` would resolve it.

content.text:
`0.62.0-3` change set is ready (137 lines in audit.md, 41 in max-review-1.md, 15 in chores-12.md). I verified every remaining `--por`/`--dual` reference:

- **Audit findings `## 1`–`## 8`, "Today/Surface/Gap" bullets, Defaults "Today's flag" column** — describe today's code. Correctly kept `--por`.
- **Decisions + Gap #3** — intentional "replaces `--por`/`--dual`" framing. Kept by design.

**One borderline group I left alone — your call:** the **"Equalization candidates, ranked"** section (lines 445-461, from the commonality pass) still proposes "make `--por` / `--dual` peer flags" — the design the `--mode` decision superseded. I left it because the doc's Status note says the commonality analysis is a preserved historical record. But it now reads as proposing the rejected design. Two options:

- **(a) Leave it** — it's analysis-stage history; the authoritative Decisions/Gaps reflect `--mode`.
- **(b) Update it** — add a one-line "(superseded by `--mode`, see Decisions)" note so a reader isn't misled, without rewriting the analysis.

I'd lean **(b)** — a light superseded-note, not a rewrite.

What's in the commit otherwise:
- audit.md: `--mode=<single|dual>` across Topology Decisions, Por's view, matrix + quick-ref, Mapping section, Gap #3, Gap #11, CLI-flag layer, escape-hatch; Privacy cross-ref reframed.
- max-review-1.md: Concern #2 trimmed to Privacy-only (Topology half resolved); Disposition row updated.
- chores-12.md: 0.62.0-3 narrative bullet.

Want (a) or (b) on the candidates, then I commit `0.62.0-3`?

file.content:
- **`--private` errors when category != github-ish**
  (not silent-ignore). Surfaces footgun cases like
  `--repo local=... --private`.
- **Uniform across dual sides**. `--repo` applies to
  both code and `.claude/` companion (today's behavior).
  Per-side `--repo-code` / `--repo-bot` deferred to a
  future cycle if a real need surfaces.
- **Remote-create-fail recovery flag** (`--remote-
  provisioned` or similar) deferred to a follow-up
  cycle. Today's abort-on-failure is acceptable.
- **Default: `github-create`** (today's behavior;
  natural-default axis). Resolution chain ends in
  baked-in default for the category; account / URL
  prefix still error at Global if unset (user-specific
  keys, no natural default possible).

#### Privacy

- **States** — `public` (default) | `private`.
- **Today** — `--private` flag.
- **Surface** — `--private`.
- **Scope** — only meaningful when Remote = `github-create`.
- **Gap** — none structurally; the flag is independent
  and orthogonal.

**Decisions (0.61.0):**

- **Default: `public`** (preserves today's behavior).
  User explicitly chose default-public over both
  strict-explicit-required and default-private; rationale
  is the user's personal context (no paid private plan)
  and back-compat. Consistent with the broader "defaults
  where natural" principle.
- **Add `--public` peer flag**. `--private` / `--public`
  exactly-one-of. Needed because `[default].private =
  true` is settable in user-config, so a per-invocation
  spelling for "public" must exist. (Carries the same
  exactly-one-of tension Topology had before `--mode`;
  whether Privacy adopts a parallel `--visibility=
  <private|public>` value flag is still open.)

content.text:
GitHub **repository visibility** at creation time — whether the repo `init`/`clone` provisions on GitHub is public or private.

From the Privacy axis (audit.md:645-668):
- **States**: `public` (default) | `private`.
- **Today's flag**: `--private`.
- **Scope**: only meaningful when Remote = `github-create` (if you're not creating a GitHub remote, it's moot).
- **Default `public`**: your explicit choice — no paid private plan + back-compat.

The Concern #2 Privacy half is that `--private` / `--public` has the same "exactly-one-of with a `[default].private` default" contradiction Topology had. The parallel fix would be one value flag.

Naming note: since this is GitHub visibility, `--visibility=<private|public>` is reasonable, but if we want to mirror `--mode`'s short style you might prefer `--vis=` or even fold it into a broader flag. Worth deciding when we actually take the Privacy half — it's still open, not something we have to settle now.

| Subcommand   | ... | Priv | ... |
| init <TARGET>| ... |  ✓   | ... |
| clone <URL>  | ... |  ✓   | ... |
| push <BM>    | ... |  —   | ... |
  ...(all runtime: —)
```

It's scoped to when Remote = `github-create`.

But your init-only instinct is worth pinning down — it's a real design question. Visibility sets a *new* GitHub repo's public/private. `init` clearly creates a new repo. `clone` clones an *existing* one (which already has its visibility). So the only way `--visibility` earns its place on `clone` is if clone ever **creates** a remote — e.g., provisioning a brand-new `.claude/` companion repo for a workspace that didn't have one. If clone only ever clones pre-existing repos, then `--visibility` on clone is dead surface and Privacy should be **init-only**.

So the question back to you: does `clone` create any remote, or only clone existing ones? If only-clone, I'd drop Privacy (and arguably the whole Remote-create axis) from the clone row. Want me to dig into `clone`'s actual behavior in the code to settle it?

content.text:
Confirmed — you're right, **Privacy is init-only today**:

- `init -h`: `--private` ("Create private GitHub repos (default: public)").
- `clone -h`: only `--por` + utility flags — **no `--private`, no `--account`, no `--repo`**.
- `push -h`: none (expected).

And `clone` takes a `<TARGET>` to clone *from* — it clones existing repos (deriving the bot URL for dual), it doesn't **provision** new remotes. So it has no reason to set visibility.

**This means the matrix is wrong about `clone`.** audit.md:985 marks clone `✓` for both `Priv` and `A/R`:

```
| clone <URL> | ✓ creation | ✓(A/R) | ✓(Priv) | ✓* | ✓ | ✓ | — |
```

But clone today has neither, and by its nature (clone existing, don't create) it shouldn't gain them. The matrix is the "post-equalization target," so this is the target being overstated — clone's row should read `A/R —` and `Priv —` (only `T`, `CP*`, `CFG`, `NO-CFG` apply).

So two corrections fall out:

1. **Privacy half (Concern #2)** lands as `--visibility=public|private`, default `public`, **`init`-only**.
2. **Matrix fix**: clone `Priv ✓ → —`, and `A/R ✓ → —` (clone resolves its source URL from the TARGET, it doesn't use the Remote-*create* axis).

Both are design-doc edits discoverable from your check. Want me to fold them into a `0.62.0-4` (Privacy half + matrix correction together), or keep them separate? And should I verify clone's no-provisioning behavior in `clone.rs` before correcting the matrix, so we're sure?

lastPrompt: Yes -4 but I just noticed Cargo.toml has been updated, let's do that moving forward, I'm leaning towards squashing this, but we'll see. Thoughts

content.text:
Clone verified — `clone.rs` has **no provisioning** (no `gh repo create`, no `--private`); it only does `jj git clone --colocate` for code and `.claude`. So it clones existing remotes, never creates them. That nails it: Privacy and Remote-create are genuinely `init`-only, and the matrix's clone row is overstated. `0.62.0-4` will fix it.

**On Cargo.toml** — agreed to bump per-commit going forward. Why it lagged: `-1/-2/-3` are purely-docs, and the protocol lets docs commits skip the cargo cycle (no install → no bump), so Cargo.toml sat at `0.62.0-0`. Bumping per-commit keeps `vc-x1 --version` honest about which `-N` you're on — the cost is running `cargo install` on each (otherwise lock/binary drift). I'll bump `-4` to `0.62.0-4`. The `-1/-2/-3` gap I'd leave (or it's moot if we squash — see below).

**On squashing — one thing to weigh before you decide.** The seed-then-delete-per-item structure we built (`-1` seeds `max-review-1.md`, `-2` deletes #1, `-3` trims #2, `-4` next…) exists *specifically* to show the review list shrinking over time in git — your original "see changes over time" goal.

- **Squash collapses that.** The single `0.62.0` commit would show `max-review-1.md` only in its final trimmed state and `audit.md` only in its final state. The per-item evolution — the whole reason for the structure — vanishes from `main`.
- **Merge non-ff** (what we did for `0.61.0`) *preserves* every `-N` commit (reachable via the merge's second parent), so `git log max-review-1.md` shows seed → −#1 → trim-#2 → … over time.

So squashing would undercut the structure we deliberately built. If the over-time record matters, merge non-ff is the consistent close-out; if you only care about a clean single entry on `main`, squash is fine but the per-item decomposition was wasted. No need to decide now — it's a close-out call — but worth knowing the per-item commits and the squash are at cross-purposes.

Proceed with `0.62.0-4` (Privacy half `--visibility=public|private` init-only + matrix clone-row fix + Cargo bump to `0.62.0-4`)?

message.content: From my PoV we're doing these one by one so to just keep the order nice, in the end max-review-1.md should be "empty" and we'll delete it. But if we should commit this series as-is we should then go back and update Cargo.toml.  Doesn't jj automatically rebase if we directly edit an existing node without create an empty "@"?

content.text:
Two confirmations + a convention check:

**No squash / per-item / end empty** — got it, that's consistent: merge non-ff at close-out preserves every `-N`, `max-review-1.md` shrinks to empty and gets deleted in the final step, and git (via the merge) holds the whole evolution. Good plan.

**jj auto-rebase** — yes, exactly right. `jj edit <rev>` then modifying the files rewrites that commit *and automatically rebases all descendants* onto the new version. No need to make an empty `@` first. (Afterward `@` sits *on* the edited commit, per the CLAUDE.md "post-amend" note; I'd `jj new` at the tip when done to get a clean working copy.)

Before I retroactively bump `-1/-2/-3`, let me confirm the actual convention from `0.61.0` history rather than guess.

45	- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
46	  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
47	  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
48	  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
49	  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
50	  elegantly.
51	- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
52	  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
53	  concrete equalization that validates the
54	  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
55	- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
56	  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
57	  itself for in 0.65.0.
58	
59	## Concerns
60	
61	### 1. Runtime `--por` semantics — doc text vs. intent
62	
63	**Original critique** (max, 2026-05-26): The Topology
64	Decisions block at `audit.md:530-534` reads:
65	
66	> `--por` at runtime short-circuits `default_scope` →
67	> **`Scope([Code])`** regardless of what the workspace
68	> says. Escape hatch for "this command, just my code,
69	> leave `.claude/` alone."
70	
71	The bot read that as code-privileged: creation-time
72	`--por` means "this workspace is single," runtime `--por`
73	means "ignore the bot side this invocation." Two
74	different operations mounted on one spelling — felt
75	overloaded.
76	
77	**User clarification** (wink, 2026-05-26): runtime `--por`
78	isn't side-specific. It means **"treat the repo I'm
79	pointed at (`.` by default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as a
80	single repo — no sibling lookup."** Symmetric across
81	code and bot:
82	
83	- `--por -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate on code,
84	  no sibling.
85	- `--por -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`, no sibling.
86	- `--por -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate on that
87	  repo as-is.
88	
89	Creation-time `--por` is a special case of the same rule
90	(the target doesn't exist yet, so "treat as standalone"
91	collapses to "create as standalone"). One rule, two life
92	stages.
93	
94	**Bot retraction:** the "two meanings on one spelling"
95	critique evaporates under this framing. Also: the bot's
96	proposed alternative ("use `--scope code` instead") was
97	wrong — it conflates *selecting one side of a known dual
98	workspace* (`--scope`) with *declaring the target isn't
99	dual* (`--por`). Different mental models, not
100	interchangeable.
101	
102	**Status:** **resolved on framing; doc-text update
103	pending.** The audit doc currently codifies the narrower
104	code-asymmetric reading at two spots:
105	
106	- `audit.md:530-534` (Topology Decisions, "runtime
107	  override" bullet) — says `→ Scope([Code])`, "just my
108	  code."
109	- `audit.md:897-913` (Por's view of the chain, "Runtime
110	  `--por`" bullet) — says "short-circuits
111	  `default_scope` → `Scope([Code])` and ignores
112	  `.claude/`."
113	
114	**Action:** at the next pickup of this design (likely
115	0.62.0 Preparation), rewrite both spots to spell out the
116	target-relative semantics. Suggested text for the
117	Topology bullet:
118	
119	> Runtime override on every subcommand: `--por` declares
120	> the target repo (default `.`, or `-R/--repo <path>`)
121	> as single — no sibling traversal, no `.vc-config.toml
122	> > other-repo` lookup, no cross-repo ochid validation.
123	> Symmetric across code and bot — `--por` doesn't
124	> privilege a side, it suppresses the sibling-discovery
125	> step.
126	
127	### 2. "Exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" is contradictory phrasing
128	
129	**Critique:** `audit.md:521-522` says clap-enforced
130	exactly-one-of `--por` / `--dual`; `:525` says
131	default-dual. If neither flag is passed, either clap
132	rejects (true exactly-one-of, friction for the common
133	case) or it accepts (default kicks in, "exactly-one-of"
134	is misleading). Same shape on `--private` / `--public`
135	at `:643-647`.
136	
137	**Status:** open.
138	
139	**Proposed action:** spell it "**at most one of**;
140	absence → workspace-config or baked-in default." Honest
141	about what the default does. Same fix for Privacy. Docs-
142	only edit, no code implications.
143	
144	### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability
145	
146	**Critique:** `copying.md:29-38` defines six flags:
147	`--init-from`, `--init-from-code`, `--init-from-bot`,
148	and `-recursive` variants of each. But recursion is a
149	property of the *operand*, not the flag: `cp src/* dst/`
150	is recursive whether you said `-r` or not; `cp
151	src/foo.txt dst/` isn't. The shell expands the glob; the
152	bot thinks vc-x1 should walk each resolved path and do
153	the right thing.
154	
155	**Proposed action:** one flag per side.
156	`--init-from-code=<glob>` does the right thing per
157	resolved path (file → file copy; dir → recursive copy).
158	Cuts the surface in half, matches `cp` semantics users
159	already have in their head. The `-recursive` naming
160	muddies what the flag controls vs. what the operand
161	controls.
162	
163	**Status:** open. Material to copying.md design before
164	that cycle starts.
165	
166	### 4. `--repo none` × Topology interaction isn't spelled out
167	
168	**Critique:** the Remote axis Decisions
169	(`audit.md:596-624`) introduces `--repo none` as a peer
170	category, with `--account` skipped under it. Fine in
171	isolation. But `--repo none` × `--dual` means "dual
172	workspace with no remote for either side." The
173	bot/sync/push workflow assumes remote-backed `.claude/`.
174	The doc doesn't say whether dual+no-remote is
175	first-class or rejected at parse time.
176	
177	**Proposed action:** add one paragraph to the Remote
178	axis (or matrix footnotes) on the Topology × Remote=none
179	interaction. Don't leave it implicit. The bot thinks a
180	future user trying `init --dual --repo none` will hit a
181	confused error later in the workflow.
182	
183	**Status:** open. Affects implementation when `--repo
184	none` lands (Gap #5).
185	
186	### 5. List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" semantics deserves an explicit callout
187	
188	**Critique:** `audit.md:697-699` says: "If user passes
189	any `--init-from*` on CLI, the config-pinned list is
190	ignored — match the broader chain rule."
191	
192	The "broader chain rule" is about *scalar* values where
193	"wins" unambiguously means "replaces." For list-valued

file.content:
#### Privacy

- **States** — `public` (default) | `private`.
- **Today** — `--private` flag.
- **Surface** — `--private`.
- **Scope** — only meaningful when Remote = `github-create`.
- **Gap** — none structurally; the flag is independent
  and orthogonal.

**Decisions (0.61.0):**

- **Default: `public`** (preserves today's behavior).
  User explicitly chose default-public over both
  strict-explicit-required and default-private; rationale
  is the user's personal context (no paid private plan)
  and back-compat. Consistent with the broader "defaults
  where natural" principle.
- **Add `--public` peer flag**. `--private` / `--public`
  exactly-one-of. Needed because `[default].private =
  true` is settable in user-config, so a per-invocation
  spelling for "public" must exist. (Carries the same
  exactly-one-of tension Topology had before `--mode`;
  whether Privacy adopts a parallel `--visibility=
  <private|public>` value flag is still open.)

content.text: Applying the Privacy axis redesign (`--visibility`, init-only).

input.old_string:
- **Add `--public` peer flag**. `--private` / `--public`
  exactly-one-of. Needed because `[default].private =
  true` is settable in user-config, so a per-invocation
  spelling for "public" must exist. (Carries the same
  exactly-one-of tension Topology had before `--mode`;
  whether Privacy adopts a parallel `--visibility=
  <private|public>` value flag is still open.)
input.new_string:
- **Visibility via `--visibility=<public|private>`** —
  one value-bearing flag, mirroring the Topology `--mode`
  decision (a single flag can't conflict with itself, so
  no "exactly-one-of" enforcement). Default `public`.
  Needed because `[default].private = true` is settable
  in user-config, so a per-invocation spelling for
  "public" must exist; `--visibility=public` provides it.
  `--private` / `--public` kept as optional boolean
  shortcuts (back-compat — `--private` exists today).
- **`init`-only**. Visibility sets a newly-provisioned
  GitHub repo's public/private, so it's meaningless on
  `clone` (clones existing repos, never provisions) and
  on every runtime subcommand.

**Footnotes:**

- `clone CP*` — copying on `clone` overlays *on top of*
  the cloned content (same flag set, same collision
  rules as `init`).
- `push SC*` — push uses bookmark scoping rather than
  `--scope`; the cell marks "yes, push reads scope-ish
  info" without implying surface identity.
- `validate-desc` / `fix-desc` `SC*` — these are the
  desc-family outliers that bypass `for_each_repo`
  today (per `## Commonality`); post-equalization
  they route through scope like the rest of the
  `CommonArgs` family.
- `config dump` is a new subcommand to be added — emits

### 2. Privacy `--private` / `--public` — same "exactly-one-of with default" tension

**Topology half — resolved (0.62.0-3).** The original
critique flagged the contradictory "exactly-one-of
`--por` / `--dual` with default-dual" phrasing. Rather
than reword it, `0.62.0-3` replaced the two flags with a
single `--mode=<single|dual>` value flag — a single flag
can't conflict with itself, so the contradiction is gone.

**Privacy half — open.** The Privacy axis carries the
identical shape: `--private` / `--public` exactly-one-of
with a `[default].private` default (audit.md Privacy
Decisions). If neither is passed, either clap rejects
(friction) or the default kicks in ("exactly-one-of" is
misleading).

**Status:** open (Privacy half).

**Proposed action:** mirror the Topology fix — adopt a
single value flag `--visibility=<private|public>`
(default `public`), optionally with `--private` /
`--public` as shortcuts. Resolves the contradiction the
same way `--mode` did for Topology.

### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Max review #1 — 0.61.0 por/dual parity design

Captured 2026-05-26 (max effort) after the close-out commit
of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
disposition), or applied (already changed).

## Scope reviewed

- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).
- [`copying.md`](copying.md) — copying mechanism design
  stub from `0.61.0-4`.
- [`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md) — older
  forward-looking doc (read for context only; not
  critiqued).
- [`/notes/chores/chores-12.md > ## docs: por/dual parity
  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
  cycle narrative.

Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
durable references.

## Strengths

For balance — what the bot thinks works well:

- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality
  inversion → axis decomposition). The `-2` inversion is
  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling
  out `--por` as a bundle of six axes opens design space
  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
  a real defect of the bundle framing.
- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
  initially listed as independent; the design pass
  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
  elegantly.
- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
  concrete equalization that validates the
  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
  itself for in 0.65.0.

## Concerns

### 3. `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface for the same capability

**Critique:** `copying.md:29-38` defines six flags:
`--init-from`, `--init-from-code`, `--init-from-bot`,
and `-recursive` variants of each. But recursion is a
property of the *operand*, not the flag: `cp src/* dst/`
is recursive whether you said `-r` or not; `cp
src/foo.txt dst/` isn't. The shell expands the glob; the
bot thinks vc-x1 should walk each resolved path and do
the right thing.

**Proposed action:** one flag per side.
`--init-from-code=<glob>` does the right thing per
resolved path (file → file copy; dir → recursive copy).
Cuts the surface in half, matches `cp` semantics users
already have in their head. The `-recursive` naming
muddies what the flag controls vs. what the operand
controls.

**Status:** open. Material to copying.md design before
that cycle starts.

### 4. `--repo none` × Topology interaction isn't spelled out

**Critique:** the Remote axis Decisions
(`audit.md:596-624`) introduces `--repo none` as a peer
category, with `--account` skipped under it. Fine in
isolation. But `--repo none` × `--dual` means "dual
workspace with no remote for either side." The
bot/sync/push workflow assumes remote-backed `.claude/`.
The doc doesn't say whether dual+no-remote is
first-class or rejected at parse time.

**Proposed action:** add one paragraph to the Remote
axis (or matrix footnotes) on the Topology × Remote=none
interaction. Don't leave it implicit. The bot thinks a
future user trying `init --dual --repo none` will hit a
confused error later in the workflow.

**Status:** open. Affects implementation when `--repo
none` lands (Gap #5).

### 5. List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" semantics deserves an explicit callout

**Critique:** `audit.md:697-699` says: "If user passes
any `--init-from*` on CLI, the config-pinned list is
ignored — match the broader chain rule."

The "broader chain rule" is about *scalar* values where
"wins" unambiguously means "replaces." For list-valued
axes, the bot thinks users coming from `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`
/ `PATH` will expect merge, not replace. The replace
choice is defensible (predictability beats expressivity)
but it's not the obvious choice.

**Proposed action:** keep the replace semantics, but add
an explicit "list-typed-axis rule" sentence — "for
list-valued axes like Copying, CLI replaces config; to
merge, pass the config globs on CLI too." Prevents the
same conversation in 0.62.0.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

### 6. Gap-list ordering hides one structural dependency

**Critique:** `audit.md:1010-1080` ranks gaps by "blast
radius." But Gap #7 (`default_scope` broken-dual
detection) is a *prerequisite* for Gap #9 (copying
mechanism), per `copying.md:81-92` — copying defers
validation to the first downstream subcommand, which
means broken-dual detection has to exist first.

**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
"ranking is rough; some structural prerequisites (e.g.
#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
renumbering.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

## Nits

Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
  hardening a use case nobody asked for.

- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.

- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
  get consulted?") — a different angle.

- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2 | Privacy `--private`/`--public` exactly-one-of (Topology half resolved via `--mode`, 0.62.0-3) | Open | `audit.md` Privacy Decisions |
| 3 | `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface | Open | `copying.md` Surface section |
| 4 | `--repo none` × Topology interaction | Open | `audit.md` Remote Decisions or matrix footnote |
| 5 | List-valued CLI-vs-config "wins" | Open | `audit.md` Copying Decisions |
| 6 | Gap-list ordering hides one prereq | Open | `audit.md` Gap list intro |
| N1 | `finalize` matrix row footnote | Open | `audit.md` matrix |
| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | Open | `audit.md` top |
| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | Open | `audit.md` § "Por's view" |
| N4 | `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` topology-blind note | Open | `audit.md` (anywhere appropriate) |
| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |

## TL;DR

Items 1 and 3 are the bot's highest-conviction concerns —
the runtime `--por` semantics doc (now resolved on
framing but doc text still needs the rewrite) and the
`--init-from-recursive` surface doubling. The rest are
docs-only edits that don't block 0.62.0 starting on T7,
but each is cheaper to apply now (single design pass)
than after the next ten cycles have referenced the
current text.

# References

[1]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md
[2]: /notes/design-cli/copying.md
[3]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity.md
[4]: /notes/chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610

| 3 | `--init-from-recursive` doubles the surface | Open | `copying.md` Surface section |
| 4 | `--repo none` × Topology interaction | Open | `audit.md` Remote Decisions or matrix footnote |
